parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, three-player bargaining game centers on two chip types: a common-value GREEN (0.50 to everyone) and a privately-valued RED (dog=0.30, buffalo=0.10, cat=0.40). Because GREEN is worth more to every player than a single RED, mutually profitable trades require exchanging unequal quantities (for example, 1 GREEN for 2 RED can increase value for both sides when the recipient values RED relatively highly or values it less but holds many RED). Buffalo, who values RED the least (0.10), is the most eager seller of RED and the natural counterparty for offers that convert GREEN into multiple REDs. Cat, who assigns the highest value to RED (0.40), demands favorable exchange rates to part with GREEN, whereas dog (RED=0.30) sits in between and will accept modestly favorable exchanges. Strategic uncertainty comes from (1) proposers not knowing others' private RED valuations, (2) multiple acceptances producing a random recipient, and (3) changing holdings across turns that constrain feasible offers. Given the player personas, dog (cooperative, stable) will repeatedly propose straightforward exchanges that look mutually beneficial on paper; buffalo (exploitation-cautious, structure-seeking) prefers trades that clearly improve his own value and will sell RED when it is obviously profitable; cat (structure-seeking, tolerant of inequality) will propose larger-quantity exchanges when those moves raise his private value. Observationally, we expect several simple 1-for-2-style exchanges (1 GREEN offered to buy 2 RED) to clear when buffalo is the counterparty, occasional multi-RED-for-multi-GREEN offers targeting dog, and some declined offers late in the game when feasible counterparties lack the required chips.

rounds.round_explanation: Round 0 is the information-gathering round. Proposers test likely counterparties with small, Pareto-improving deals (e.g., 1 GREEN for 2 RED). Buffalo's low RED value makes him a natural acceptor of such deals, and early acceptance reveals both willingness to trade RED. Multiple-acceptance risk exists when both non-senders gain from the swap, producing random matching.

Players update beliefs and holdings. Dog and cat have seen buffalo accept RED-for-GREEN exchanges and continue to target him when feasible. Buffalo, having acquired some GREEN, will still sell RED when the exchange is clearly beneficial; when both non-senders can gain, multiple accepts are possible and a random recipient will be chosen. By the end of this round holdings shift noticeably toward cat and dog accumulating RED and buffalo accumulating GREEN.

By round 2 some players have depleted the chip type others most want to sell (buffalo has fewer RED to sell), which reduces the space of mutually beneficial swaps. Proposers test remaining profitable margins but face more frequent declines because counterparties either lack the requested chips or would lose value by trading. We therefore expect more declined proposals in the final round.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three-player repeated bilateral bargaining where green chips are universally worth $0.50 and red chips differ by player (badger values red at $0.80; otter and goose value red at $0.30). Each proposer can request one color and offer another; responders accept only if their private valuations make the exchange profitable. Incentives: otter and goose prefer trading their low-value red for higher-value green (+$0.20 per 1-for-1 swap), while badger prefers acquiring red (gains +$0.30 per 1-for-1 swap). This creates a persistent pattern: otter and goose will propose swapping red for green (to increase their own value) and badger will often accept those offers. Conversely, proposals by badger to buy red by offering green are unattractive to otter and goose and are likely to be declined. Uncertainty arises because each player sees only their own values; proposers may misestimate who will accept and whether multiple acceptances will create random allocation. Given the personas (otter and goose are empathic/reflective but cautious; badger is patient and inequality-tolerant), we forecast repeated small 1-for-1 red->green trades by otter and goose that badger accepts, and repeated failed attempts by badger to buy red at 1-for-1.

rounds.round_explanation: Early play: all players start with 10 red and 10 green. Otter and goose prefer green to red and have incentive to trade red for green; badger prefers red and will accept fair 1-for-1 exchanges that increase her value. Expect otter and goose proposals to be accepted by badger, while badger's attempts to buy red by offering green will be declined by others.

Midgame: holdings have shifted toward more red for badger and more green for otter/goose. The same incentive pattern persists: otter and goose continue to propose red->green 1-for-1 swaps that badger finds profitable; badger's proposals to acquire additional red by offering green remain unattractive to others and are likely declined.

Endgame: badger has accumulated several extra red chips and fewer greens but still finds 1-for-1 red acquisitions worthwhile until green becomes scarce. Otter and goose continue safe, incremental trades of red for green to raise their own payoff. Badger's direct proposals to buy red remain unattractive and are declined; otter/goose proposals continue to be accepted by badger.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players repeatedly bargain over two chip types (RED and GREEN) across three rounds, with private valuations driving who wants which chips. All players value GREEN at $0.50. RED is privately valued: peacock-high ($0.80), otter-neutral ($0.50), badger-low ($0.20). This creates clear trade asymmetries: peacock is willing to give up GREEN to acquire RED; badger is willing to give up RED to acquire GREEN; otter is ambivalent between colors. Because proposals consist of asking for one color and offering another, efficient bilateral trades typically exchange RED from badger to peacock in return for GREEN from peacock — mutually profitable given private values — while otter often plays the middle role and only accepts near-even or slightly favorable terms. Uncertainty arises because players observe only offers and holdings, not others' private valuations; responders therefore infer preferences from prior moves. Persona priors push behavior toward repeated small, transparent exchanges: peacock often proposes trades that convert GREEN into RED (profitably for her), badger proposes trades that convert RED into GREEN (profitably for him), and otter makes conservative, fairness-leaning proposals and tends to decline strictly loss-making deals. Because acceptances are simultaneous, proposers craft offers targeted to one partner (making the trade attractive for that partner while not attractive for the third), avoiding multi-acceptance randomness when possible. The sequence below forecasts plausible proposals, private accept/decline responses, and the realized single acceptances that execute each trade.

rounds.round_explanation: Early bargaining reveals private asymmetries. Otter (neutral on RED vs GREEN) opens with a simple 1-for-1 swap that is attractive to peacock (who values RED more) but unattractive to badger (who values RED less). Peacock then exploits her higher RED value by offering small GREEN payments to buy extra RED from badger. Badger reciprocates by selling RED for GREEN to whichever partner offers the best terms. Each proposer targets one partner with a profitable bilateral deal rather than proposing terms that would attract both acceptors.

With some history revealed, proposers increasingly target the most favorable counterparty. Otter remains conservative and repeats a small 1-for-1 swap aimed at peacock (who still benefits). Peacock again offers to convert a little GREEN into multiple REDs, targeting badger who is willing to sell RED. Badger in turn proposes a simple 1-for-1 exchange attractive to peacock. The pattern (peacock buys RED, badger buys GREEN) intensifies predictable bilateral matches rather than multi-acceptance competition.

Endgame dynamics favor quick, high-value conversions. Otter continues safe 1-for-1 proposals that typically attract peacock; peacock repeatedly converts scarce GREEN into additional RED where profitable; badger continues to trade away his relatively cheap RED in exchange for GREEN. With known behavior and limited rounds left, offers remain targeted, small, and immediately acceptable to the counterparty who benefits under their private valuation.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, nine-turn bargaining game is driven by a clear cross-player valuation asymmetry: GREEN is universally worth $0.50 to everyone (common-value), while RED is worth $0.10 to cat and camel but $0.30 to buffalo. Because GREEN is strictly more valuable per unit to all players, mutually profitable trades require asymmetric exchange rates (i.e., trading multiple REDs for fewer GREENs). Buffalo, who values RED relatively more than the others, can profitably trade small quantities of GREEN for several REDs and still make the recipient better off. Cat and camel, who value RED very little, cannot credibly buy GREEN from others unless they offer many REDs — which they rarely want to do because RED has low value to them. As a result, we expect many aggressive single-unit proposals from cat/camel to be declined, while carefully sized multi-unit proposals by buffalo (selling a GREEN in exchange for multiple REDs) are the most likely to be accepted. Uncertainty enters via private values (each player only knows their own valuations), private simultaneous accept/decline responses, and random selection when multiple players accept the same proposal. All players act to maximize their own total chip value at each step given their private valuation and observed public history.

rounds.round_explanation: Early probing. Cat and camel are likely to make straightforward one-for-one or modest proposals (trying to trade into more GREEN), which recipients will generally decline because GREEN is more valuable to recipients than RED. Buffalo can structure a multi-RED-for-one-GREEN offer that benefits both sides; such an offer can attract acceptances from multiple recipients early when holdings are large.

After the initial exchange, buffalo has accumulated RED and has fewer GREEN; cat and camel have slightly shifted holdings. Cat and camel may try again to acquire GREEN by offering modest amounts of RED; such proposals are attractive to buffalo (who values RED more), and buffalo can accept multiple such offers in sequence. Buffalo also continues to propose multi-RED-for-GREEN trades; when both non-senders can benefit, multiple acceptances are possible and a random recipient is chosen.

With holdings increasingly polarized (buffalo holding many RED and few GREEN; cat and camel having reduced RED), proposers continue to exploit the valuation asymmetry. Cat and camel can obtain marginal GREEN by giving up a couple REDs when buffalo is the recipient. Buffalo, constrained by dwindling GREEN stock, will still propose high-RED-for-1-GREEN trades but must respect recipients' remaining RED holdings. Random selection can determine final partners when multiple players accept.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players start with 10 red and 10 green chips each. Greens are common-value at $0.50 for everyone; reds are worth more to dog (RED=$0.90) and peacock (RED=$0.80) but not to owl (RED=$0.50). Proposers ask for one color and offer another; non-senders accept if the trade increases (or does not decrease) their private chip value. Given valuations and personas, dog and peacock repeatedly prefer to acquire reds by offering greens (they gain value per red received), while owl is indifferent between a red and a green and is the most likely partner to sell reds for greens. Because multiple acceptances trigger random recipient selection, proposers who target trades that are neutral or slightly positive for owl can reliably get a red from owl. Over three rounds we forecast a pattern: dog and peacock alternately propose one-for-one green->red swaps that owl accepts, and owl uses its proposer turns to offer red for green (a neutral-to-slightly-costly move for owl but attractive to dog/peacock), which tends to be accepted by both red-seeking players with the realized recipient selected randomly. The result concentrates reds with dog and peacock while owl accumulates greens. Uncertainty arises from random resolution when both non-senders accept and from each player only knowing their own values while observing public history.

rounds.round_explanation: Early stage: players feel out acceptance thresholds. Dog and peacock both value red more than green and will propose simple 1-for-1 green->red swaps. Owl is indifferent and likely to accept such offers. When owl proposes red->green, both dog and peacock find that attractive and accept; multiple acceptances are resolved at random.

Middle stage: pattern repeats as dog and peacock continue to try to acquire reds at 1-for-1 green cost. Owl has accumulated greens and continues to be willing to accept or to offer reds for greens; as proposer, owl's offers are attractive to both red-preferring players, producing occasional competition (randomized assignment) for the red.

End stage: with history of repeated 1-for-1 exchanges, reds concentrate with dog and peacock and greens with owl. Dog and peacock continue proposing the same favorable swap; owl's proposer turn again creates attractive offers for both non-senders, resolved randomly.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players start with equal quantities of red and green chips but different private values for red (GREEN is common-value at $0.50). Butterfly values red much more than the others (RED=$1.00) and so is the natural buyer of red; Ram and Alligator both prefer green to red (Ram RED=$0.30, Alligator RED=$0.40) and are natural sellers of red in exchange for green. The bargaining dynamic will therefore center on Butterfly repeatedly offering greens to acquire reds, and Ram/Alligator offering reds in exchange for greens. Proposers who prefer green will offer red to buy green (small, favorable exchanges for the proposer), and Butterfly will propose the opposite (offering green to buy red). Responders act only on their private values: they accept if the trade increases (or at least does not decrease) their own chip value given their private red valuation. When both non-senders would benefit, multiple acceptances create random partner selection, introducing execution risk for proposers. Over three rounds (nine proposal turns) we expect several small mutually beneficial swaps that systematically move reds toward Butterfly and greens toward Ram and Alligator, reducing Butterfly's greens and increasing her reds; trades are typically small (1 green ↔ 1 red or similar) because those are minimally costly and clearly profitable for at least one side. Uncertainty arises from (1) private information about red values during play (each player only knows their own red value), (2) random selection when multiple accept, and (3) potential strategic hesitation when trades are only weakly beneficial (zero or near-zero net gain) to some responders.

rounds.round_explanation: Early coordination: proposers test simple 1-for-1 or 1-for-2 swaps to reveal willingness to trade. Ram and Alligator, who privately prefer green, will offer red to acquire green; Butterfly will offer green to acquire red. Butterfly's high red value makes her a frequent acceptor when asked to give green in exchange for red or a frequently targeted seller of green when she proposes to buy red. When a trade is strictly profitable for one responder and neutral or mildly profitable for the other, the profitable responder is expected to accept while the other declines.

Mid-game: patterns from round 0 make Butterfly a clear buyer of red and the other two clear buyers of green. Proposers continue with small 1-for-1 offers; Butterfly often accepts offers to sell green for red (she gains or is neutral), and Ram/Alligator accept when giving red for green is beneficial. When both non-senders would gain, multiple acceptance and random partner selection continue to determine who actually trades with the proposer.

End-game: Butterfly accumulates many reds and is running low on greens but still values additional reds highly, so she continues to offer greens for reds. Ram and Alligator continue small red-for-green trades when those trades increase their own valuation. As Butterfly's green stock approaches zero, she becomes marginally less able to propose further purchases, but the pattern of red moving to Butterfly and green moving to Ram/Alligator continues.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, three-player chip exchange plays out with symmetric starting endowments (10 red, 10 green each) but asymmetric private values: all players value green at $0.50, while red is valued at $0.40 (dolphin), $0.20 (ram), and $0.80 (iguana). Because trades exchange one color for the other, profitable offers tend to be simple one-for-one swaps: a proposer who sells a red and asks for a green improves their own value if they value green more than red (dolphin and ram). Those offers will be attractive to players who value red more than green (iguana). Conversely, a proposer who sells green and asks for red will appeal to players who prefer green (dolphin and ram). Given the personas and values, the likely pattern is repeated small red->green trades proposed by dolphin and ram and accepted by iguana, while iguana's attempts to buy more red by offering green will typically be declined by dolphin and ram (they would be giving up higher-value green to get lower-value red). Uncertainty for each participant comes from not knowing others' private values; however, observed history and holdings provide strong signals (e.g., repeated acceptances by Iguana reveal a revealed preference for red). Players seek to maximize their own final portfolio value over the three rounds; they tend to make and accept small mutually beneficial swaps when those swaps increase their own private chip-value total. The overall dynamics thus produce multiple small swaps shifting green toward the two green-preferring players' portfolios and red accumulating with the red-preferring player (iguana).

rounds.round_explanation: Early signaling round: proposers have full endowments and will test simple 1-for-1 swaps. Dolphin and Ram, who privately prefer green over red, are incentivized to trade their lower-value red for higher-value green. Those offers are selectively attractive to Iguana, who values red much higher than green. Iguana is likely to accept such red->green offers; Iguana's own proposals to buy red (selling green) will be unattractive to the green-preferring players and therefore likely to be declined.

By round 1, holdings and the public history start to reveal that Iguana accepts red-for-green swaps. Dolphin and Ram continue to prefer improving their portfolios by trading red for green; they will repeat modest 1-for-1 offers. Iguana, having accumulated additional red, continues to accept those offers because each acceptance increases Iguana's private value. Iguana's own attempts to buy red by offering green remain unattractive to the others and are likely to be declined.

Final round incentives push players to capture remaining value-improving swaps. Dolphin and Ram still prefer green, so they propose further red->green 1-for-1 trades; Iguana still benefits from taking red in exchange for green and accepts while the others decline. Iguana's final attempt to buy red with green remains unattractive and is declined. The outcome is a concentration of red with Iguana and more greens held by Dolphin and Ram.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, three-player chip exchange pits two players (owl-yellow-7660 and iguana-red-1137) who privately value GREEN more than RED (GREEN=0.50, RED=0.30 or 0.10) against one player (whale-blue-4588) who privately values RED more than GREEN (RED=0.80, GREEN=0.50). Proposers can only ask for one color and offer a different color; responses are private and simultaneous. Given the private values, proposers who prefer GREEN will likely offer RED in exchange for GREEN; the whale will typically accept those offers because trading GREEN for RED raises her private value. By contrast, when the whale proposes to buy RED by selling GREEN, the other two players would have to give up a higher-valued GREEN to get a lower-valued RED, so they will typically decline. Because players only know their own values, proposers will favor simple, low-quantity offers likely to increase both their own payoff and (when there is a mutual gain) the recipient's payoff; recipients will accept only if the trade increases their private total. Random selection among multiple acceptors is possible but unlikely here because value asymmetries usually make only the whale a willing counterparty for RED-for-GREEN swaps. Overall, expect repeated small exchanges where owl and iguana sell RED to acquire GREEN (accepted by whale), and whale's attempts to buy RED by selling GREEN are typically declined.

rounds.round_explanation: Initial holdings: each player starts with 10 RED and 10 GREEN. Early dynamics favor proposals that trade low-value chips for high-value chips. Owl and Iguana both prefer GREEN and so will offer RED-for-GREEN swaps; the whale prefers RED and will accept such offers. The whale's own offers to buy RED by selling GREEN are unlikely to be accepted because others prefer GREEN.

After round 0 the whale has fewer GREEN and more RED, making her an attractive counterparty for further RED->GREEN offers. Owl and Iguana will likely repeat the same 1-for-1 swaps because they are safe, mutually beneficial against the whale's preferences. The whale's own proposals remain unattractive to the other two and are likely to fail.

A persistent pattern emerges: owl and iguana keep swapping their lower-valued RED for GREEN one-for-one, and the whale continues to accept those offers while her own GREEN->RED proposals remain unattractive. With private valuations constant across rounds, expect another repetition of the same safe, small trades.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players begin with equal nominal holdings (10 red, 10 green) but different private valuations for red (duck=0.20, gorilla=0.20, kangaroo=0.60) while green is a public common value (0.50). That creates a clear division of incentives: kangaroo is a persistent net demander of red (red > green for them), while duck and gorilla prefer green to red and are natural sellers of red. Because players see only their own private values, early proposals also serve as information signals and cheap tests: proposers will typically make small, incremental exchanges (low quantities) that are best responses given their valuations and that are likely to be accepted by at least one partner. Responders accept only if the specific exchange increases their own private value (they evaluate the trade using their private red value and the public green value). Expect many 1-for-1 or 2-for-1 style trades where duck/gorilla offer red to acquire green and kangaroo offers green to acquire red; acceptances will often come from the player who values the incoming chip more, and when multiple acceptances occur a random recipient is selected. Because preferences are stable and gains from trade are local, bargaining converges toward allocations with kangaroo accumulating red and duck/gorilla accumulating green through several small, beneficial swaps.

rounds.round_explanation: Opening round: proposers test partners with small exchanges. Duck and gorilla both prefer green and will offer red to get green; kangaroo prefers red and will offer green. Early duck offers are likely too greedy for others and decline; gorilla will successfully trade a 1-for-1 with kangaroo; kangaroo will then propose a slightly larger 2-for-1 that both non-senders find profitable, producing more red for kangaroo.

By round 1 players have mild private information revealed by prior trades. Duck continues to swap red for green in small steps; gorilla repeats a conservative 1-for-1 trade to get green; kangaroo again proposes a multi-red-for-single-green exchange to accumulate red. Acceptances follow individual gains: kangaroo accepts offers that turn green into higher-valued red, duck/gorilla accept offers that convert low-valued red into green.

Final round: patterns stabilize. Duck continues modest red→green requests that kangaroo (the red-preferrer) accepts; gorilla repeats 1-for-1 green purchases from red; kangaroo again uses small multi-red-for-one-green proposals to further concentrate red. Because gains are local and repeated offers are small, most proposals that transfer value for the recipient are accepted; when both outsiders would accept, a random selection determines which partner actually trades.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players share identical private valuations (RED = $0.80, GREEN = $0.50) and identical starting endowments (10 RED, 10 GREEN). Because every proposed trade exchanges one color for the other, any deal that benefits the proposer necessarily makes the responder worse off by the same amount (zero-sum between proposer and recipient under linear per-chip values). Given the rule that responders act to maximize their own chip-value and the players' reported tendencies (resource-protective duck, cooperation-oriented dolphin, inequality-tolerant swan), the dominant equilibrium expectation is that proposers will try to trade GREEN for RED to increase their own value, and responders will reject those offers because doing so would reduce their own value. There is strategic uncertainty early on about whether any player will propose a transfer that knowingly benefits a responder (which would be dominated for the proposer) or whether a responder with lenient fairness/preferences might accept a small loss; however, the value-maximization objective implies responders will not accept negative-value trades. Over three rounds each player has three proposal opportunities; we forecast that proposers will attempt advantageous RED-for-GREEN trades but that all nine proposals will be rejected, leaving holdings unchanged across the game.

rounds.round_explanation: Early-round dynamics: proposers are likely to test simple, profitable-for-them exchanges (request RED in return for GREEN). Because every player values RED more than GREEN, any such offer benefits the sender and harms any accepting recipient. Responders who maximize their own value will therefore decline. Expect bargaining attempts but no executed trades in round 0.

Mid-game considerations: proposers may adjust quantities to try to entice acceptance (e.g., smaller seller quantity or larger demanded quantity), but any proposal that gives the responder net value would require the proposer to accept a loss. Since players are value-maximizers, responders continue to decline offers that would reduce their own payoff. Public history of declined offers increases the credibility that future proposals will also be rejected.

End-game: with limited remaining rounds, proposers might attempt bolder ratios, but any trade that materially benefits the proposer continues to hurt potential recipients. Given consistent prior rejections and identical private valuations, responders remain unwilling to accept. Expect another round of rejected proposals and no executed trades.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players repeatedly trade two chip types (RED and GREEN). GREEN has a common private value of $0.50 for everyone; RED has heterogeneous private values (pig 0.30, bear 0.60, hamster 1.00). Proposers will generally request the chip they value more and offer the chip they value less. That makes trades where a low-red-valuer (pig) gives RED to receive GREEN attractive to pig and also attractive to high-red-valuers (bear, hamster) who want RED and are willing to give GREEN if the exchange is favorable. Because responses are private and acceptance is by individual choice, proposers cannot pick partners; they design offers to attract at least one acceptor. Pig, being cooperation-oriented and having the lowest value on RED, is the most frequent acceptor of offers that ask for RED in exchange for GREEN. Bear (moderate RED value) is selective and only accepts when the exchange is close to break-even or favors them; hamster (highest RED value) resists giving RED unless the price is very favorable but will reliably buy RED by offering GREEN because that increases their own value. Uncertainty arises from private valuations and the random tie-break if multiple accept. Over three rounds, the most likely pattern is that proposals to buy RED by offering GREEN get accepted by pig repeatedly, while pig's attempts to buy GREEN by offering RED are typically declined by the other two.

rounds.round_explanation: Start-of-game holdings are symmetric. Pig (low RED value) wants GREEN over RED but others do not find simple 1-for-1 exchanges favorable. Early successful trades will be those where proposers offer GREEN to buy RED: pig is the natural acceptor because pig values GREEN more than RED. Bear will attempt a modest exchange to acquire more RED from pig; hamster will similarly try to buy RED but only pig accepts.

After the first round pig has accumulated some GREEN and others have adjusted holdings. Bear continues the profitable strategy of offering GREEN to buy RED at about 1-for-1, which pig again finds attractive. Hamster, who highly values RED, repeats buying RED by offering GREEN; pig remains the frequent acceptor. Pig's own proposals to buy GREEN by offering RED remain unattractive to the others and are likely to be declined.

Final round incentives are similar: bear and hamster both prefer to accumulate RED and will repeatedly offer GREEN to buy RED. Pig, with many GREEN chips and low valuation of RED, continues to accept those exchanges. Pig's own proposals remain unattractive and are declined. Because acceptances are private and single-acceptor trades are sufficient to execute, proposers secure RED primarily by targeting pig's comparative preference for GREEN.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players hold identical counts of two chip types but have different private values for RED (GREEN is common-value). Whale values RED highest (0.80) and will try to accumulate REDs by offering GREENS; Swan (RED=0.10) and Turtle (RED=0.20) value GREENS relatively more (GREEN=0.50) and will be willing to trade away low-value REDs for GREENS. Because players only know their own valuations, proposers will target partners who appear to have weaker valuation for the requested chips (offer generous packages of the low-value chip) while keeping offers within their holdings. Acceptance decisions are straightforward value checks: a responder accepts only if the chips they receive are worth at least as much as the chips they give up, using their private values. When multiple responders accept, a random selection determines the recipient, introducing risk for a proposer seeking a particular partner. Over three rounds with three proposal turns each, we expect early exploratory offers that test acceptance thresholds, middle-round exploitation of revealed behavior (targeting the most likely acceptor), and final-round attempts to extract remaining gains given limited future bargaining. The behavioral priors (resource-protective whale, stable/inequality-tolerant swan, logical/cooperative turtle) bias proposals and acceptance thresholds but do not override the simple private-value profit calculus.

rounds.round_explanation: Opening moves. Proposers test partner thresholds. Whale will attempt to buy RED by offering GREEN; responders reject unless the exchange improves their own value. Swan and Turtle will try to trade away low-value REDs to obtain GREENS, offering packages sized to attract the high-RED-value Whale.

Players use information revealed by previous acceptances. Whale has accumulated REDs and is still willing to trade small amounts of GREEN for more RED. Swan continues to sell REDs to buy GREENS but has reduced RED stock; Turtle continues structured, profitable swaps. Responders accept when the immediate private-value gain is positive.

Final opportunities. Whale, low on GREEN, makes tighter offers; Swan makes a larger bundled offer of REDs for a GREEN and risks multiple acceptances; Turtle makes conservative, profitable swaps. Because this is the last round, players act to maximize immediate private-value gains rather than signaling for future rounds.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players start with identical endowments but different private values for RED chips (frog: 0.60, hedgehog: 0.20, badger: 0.50). GREEN is common-value (0.50). Proposers request one color and offer another; responders accept if the trade increases their own private payoff. Because hedgehog values RED much less than GREEN, she is the most willing recipient of trades that convert RED into GREEN (she is also quite generous as a proposer). Frog values RED most and GREEN slightly less; he will try to acquire RED by offering GREEN in small, efficient one-for-one swaps that are just profitable to him and attractive to hedgehog. Badger values RED and GREEN equally and displays higher exploitation caution; she tends to accept only clearly profitable or neutral trades and often declines marginally advantageous deals. Uncertainty arises because each player observes only their own private values; players infer others' likely preferences from past actions. This produces repeated small-volume exchanges: frog repeatedly offers 1 GREEN for 1 RED (profitable to frog, attractive to hedgehog), hedgehog often offers multi-RED-for-1-GREEN (generous) that both non-senders sometimes accept, and badger acts cautiously, mainly accepting when directly profitable. Randomization resolves multi-accept situations, so proposers who make generous offers risk giving away value to a random recipient rather than a targeted partner.

rounds.round_explanation: Early round: frog (high RED value) opens by trying small 1-for-1 swaps to convert GREEN into RED; hedgehog, who dislikes RED, readily accepts. Hedghog as proposer leverages her low RED value to buy GREENs aggressively (offering 2 RED for 1 GREEN) — generous offers attract both non-senders, producing a random recipient. Badger responds cautiously but accepts clear gains (e.g., trading a GREEN for 2 REDs when offered). Holdings update between turns and inform subsequent proposals.

With updated holdings, frog repeats efficient 1-for-1 GREEN->RED requests; hedgehog again trades away RED for GREEN, sometimes offering 2 REDs per GREEN to entice partners. Badger continues to accept only clearly profitable or neutral deals (she accepts hedgehog's generous offers and declines frog's one-for-one when it would hurt her). Trades continue to be small and largely bilateral or resolved by random selection when both non-senders accept hedgehog's generous offers.

Final round: patterns stabilize. Frog continues to target RED via small exchanges; hedgehog continues to use RED as a cheap currency to buy GREEN (sometimes offering 2 REDs per GREEN), producing mutually beneficial trades for recipients and herself. Badger remains cautious but accepts hedgehog's generous proposals and declines frog's proposals when they would reduce her payoff. Random selection occasionally determines the beneficiary when multiple players accept hedgehog's generous offers.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, three-proposer bargaining game pits a common-value green chip (worth $0.50 to all) against heterogeneous private values for red chips (tiger: $0.10, mouse: $0.30, unicorn: $0.20). Each proposer can request one color and offer another in integer quantities; recipients accept only if the trade increases their own private total value. Given those values, proposers will generally try to acquire greens (highest-value chip) and dispense red chips (lowest personal value for most players). Acceptance thresholds differ: players with higher red valuations (mouse) require fewer red chips offered to compensate for giving up green; players with low red valuation (tiger) require many red chips to be compensated. Uncertainty comes from private red valuations (each player knows only their own), who will accept, and the random tie-breaking if multiple recipients accept. Using the provided personas as priors, I forecast that proposers who most benefit from acquiring greens but who can also offer enough red to make the deal attractive to at least one other player will secure trades. Mouse's relatively high red valuation makes them the most frequent accepter; tiger, with very low red value, rarely accepts giving green unless offered many red chips. Players also show tendencies consistent with structure-seeking and cautiousness: proposers often pick modest, clearly profitable exchanges (for themselves) that appear likely to be accepted given observed patterns, while some proposals serve as inexpensive price exploration and are declined.

rounds.round_explanation: Early round: everyone starts with 10 red / 10 green. Proposers are likely to request greens and offer reds. Tiger (low red value) can profitably trade away a few reds for a green and will set quantities sufficient to entice a partner; mouse (high red value) is the likeliest recipient of such offers. Some modest proposals (profitable for the proposer but not attractive enough to any recipient) will be declined as players reveal acceptance thresholds.

Mid game: public history shows mouse accepting multiple red-for-green exchanges, revealing a high red valuation to observers. Proposers capitalize on that by offering bundle sizes that are profitable for themselves yet attractive to mouse. Some smaller, self-improving offers will still be declined when they fail to meet recipients' acceptance thresholds.

Final round: holdings are skewed (mouse has accumulated many reds and fewer greens). Proposers take last chances to convert low-value reds into greens; mouse, holding many reds, can be selective and often declines small-price offers, but continues to accept deals that improve their own value. Given revealed behavior, multiple similar red-for-green offers are successful and channel additional green chips to proposers who trade away reds.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players start with equal chip counts but different private red-chip valuations (parrot low, fox high, bear medium) and identical green-chip values. This creates clear bilateral trading incentives: parrot prefers to convert low-value red into green, fox prefers to acquire red by giving up green, and bear is roughly indifferent but may act to gain small benefits or facilitate trades. Because proposers cannot choose among multiple acceptors, they tend to propose offers attractive to at least one likely counterparty rather than strongly imbalanced offers that no one will accept. Uncertainty comes from each player knowing only their own private values (so they infer others' likely preferences from prior offers and holdings) and from random tie-breaking when multiple accept. Over three rounds with one proposal per player each round, we forecast repeated small exchanges: parrot repeatedly sells red to get green, fox repeatedly purchases red by offering green, and bear opportunistically buys or sells one-for-one trades when they expect a partner to accept. The bargaining is short-horizon and myopic: players accept proposals that increase their own private value immediately, even if that leaves them with fewer trading assets later.

rounds.round_explanation: Early play: parrot (low red value) seeks green and offers small red-for-green swaps attractive to fox (high red value). Fox uses its green to buy additional red. Bear, initially neutral, waits but accepts a favorable red-for-green offer from parrot when it benefits the recipient.

Middle play: parrot continues to convert red to green at modest one-for-one rates that fox finds attractive. Fox, flush with some greens, proposes slightly larger red purchases (two red for one green) because parrot is willing to sell red cheaply. Bear remains willing to do neutral one-for-one trades and again finds parrot the most likely acceptor.

Final play: parrot has few red chips left and continues small red→green swaps while conserving scarce red. Fox pushes to acquire remaining red with a two-for-one request when feasible; parrot, preferring immediate gains, still accepts. Bear opportunistically trades one-for-one green↔red when a counterparty (fox) finds the exchange favorable.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, three-proposal-per-round game pits three players who all prefer red over green but differ in how much they prefer red (monkey: RED=0.80, buffalo: RED=0.70, goose: RED=0.70; GREEN=0.50 for all). Because values are discrete and chips must be traded in integer quantities, mutually beneficial trades exist only when the green: red exchange ratio fits between the buyer's and seller's relative valuations. In these primitives that usually means exchanges like 3 greens for 2 reds (3/2 = 1.5), which split the small differences in red value across players. Expect early rounds to exploit such discrete mutually beneficial bundles: monkey (highest red value) will try to concentrate reds by offering greens to lower-value red holders; lower-value players will sometimes sell reds in exchange for a slightly larger number of greens when the integer ratio allows both sides a small positive gain. After a few efficient discrete swaps the distribution of holdings will shift (some players end up very green-rich, others very red-rich), making further mutually beneficial trades harder because recipients lack the necessary endowments to accept the integer bundles. Behavioral priors (monkey: logical, adaptive; buffalo: cooperative and predictable; goose: exploitation-cautious, stable) imply players will propose economically sensible bundles when available and decline trades that make them worse off. Because acceptances are private and simultaneous, multi-accepts can occur and the recipient is chosen at random. Uncertainty remains about which potential partner accepts and about opponents' exact thresholds, but the small, discrete feasible-trade set makes the likely paths narrow: a few accepted 2-for-3 style trades early, then mostly declined proposals as holdings become lopsided.

rounds.round_explanation: Initial endowments are balanced (10 red, 10 green each). The main opportunity is to exploit the small differences in RED valuations via integer bundles (notably 3 greens for 2 reds). Monkey (highest RED value) is expected to initiate a 2-red-for-3-green purchase; both lower-value players are willing sellers on that bundle. That creates holdings asymmetry that enables a countervailing trade (selling reds to get greens) by buffalo and then a symmetric move by goose. Expect most early offers to be accepted if they match the 3-for-2 ratio and the candidate recipient has enough chips to trade.

Holdings are now lopsided (monkey becomes very red-heavy and low on greens; buffalo becomes green-rich; goose somewhere between). Many of the previously mutually beneficial 3-for-2 bundles require recipients to have multiple greens; monkey's low green balance means it cannot be a counterparty for some offers. Proposers will still attempt sensible bundles, but acceptance rates fall because potential recipients either lack the required chips or would be made worse off by the integer bundle. Expect several proposals that are economically sensible from the proposer's perspective to be declined.

With holdings even more polarized and fewer feasible integer bundles that help both sides, proposers typically make small or status-quo proposals that recipients decline. Players with many greens (buffalo) could in principle sell reds for greens, but the necessary counterparty either lacks greens to pay or would be worse off. Expect continued low acceptance: some small attempts to swap 1-for-1 or repeat 3-for-2 offers, but mostly declines.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players repeatedly propose bilateral chip swaps where each proposer requests a positive quantity of one color and offers a positive quantity of the other. Greens are worth more than or equal to reds for some players (GREEN=0.50 for all). Red values are heterogeneous (dog 0.30, cat 0.70, rabbit 0.50). Proposers will naturally try to trade away chips they value less for chips they value more, while responders will accept only if the swap increases (or at least does not decrease) their own private payoff. Uncertainty arises because each player knows only their own valuations and must infer others' likely responses from observed trades and the players' apparent preferences. Because acceptance is simultaneous and multiple acceptances are resolved randomly, proposers face the risk of trading with an unintended partner. Given personas, cat and rabbit are relatively willing to accept small gains and neutral trades (cat especially cooperative, rabbit tolerant and stable), while dog is more strategic and prefers trades that clearly improve her payoff. Early rounds will see simple mutually beneficial exchanges (small one-for-one swaps) and occasional rejected attempts when proposers try to extract larger quantity improvements; later rounds shift holdings, which changes who can profitably accept certain offers.

rounds.round_explanation: Initial holdings are balanced (10 RED, 10 GREEN each). Dog values GREEN > RED and so will propose trades offering low-value red for higher-value green; cat values RED most and will offer greens to acquire reds; rabbit is value-equal and tends to accept neutral or slightly positive offers. Early moves will test these preferences, producing straightforward one-for-one or slight-bulk exchanges and a mix of declined and accepted offers.

Holdings have shifted: cat has accumulated reds, rabbit has gained some greens. Dog continues preferring green and will try small efficient swaps (one-for-one) that both improve her payoff and are likely to be accepted. Cat continues acquiring reds using greens as currency and will post offers that multiple responders can accept; when both accept, random resolution determines the partner. Rabbit will make modest, often neutral or slightly beneficial proposals and accept favorable offers.

By the final round cat holds many reds and few greens, dog holds more greens than reds relative to her preferences, and rabbit is in between. Proposers will try small, low-friction swaps to squeeze remaining gains. Some ambitious offers that demand large quantity imbalances will be declined; modest one-for-one exchanges between complementary preferences are most likely to succeed.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players each start with 10 red and 10 green chips. Greens have a common value of $0.50 to everyone; red chips are worth $0.50 to ram and $0.40 to both squid and otter. Because two players undervalue red relative to green, bilateral swaps that convert red into green are attractive for squid and otter (they gain $0.10 per 1R->1G swap) while the mirror swap (green->red) is weak or neutral for ram (who values both equally) and unattractive for squid and otter. The dominant dynamic is that squid and otter will try to sell red to obtain green (propose buy GREEN / sell RED) to increase their own value; ram is the natural counterparty because trading a green for a red leaves ram roughly indifferent and so is the most likely to accept (especially early on). Ram, who values red as highly as green, has little private arbitrage incentive to trade, but may accept neutral swaps repeatedly. Uncertainty centers on how many indifferent accepts ram will make before conserving scarce greens, and on the randomization rule when multiple players would accept the same proposal. Over three rounds (three proposers per round), we expect most successful trades to be 1 RED -> 1 GREEN executed when ram accepts squid/otter proposals; some of ram's own proposals to trade green for red may be accepted early (when both sellers prefer green), and later ram proposals are more likely to be declined as his green stock is depleted. Behavior follows individual-payoff maximization, with squid and otter selectively proposing swaps that yield positive private gains and ram tolerating neutral swaps early on.

rounds.round_explanation: Initial endowments are balanced. Early in the game squid and otter have incentives to trade their undervalued red for green (each 1R->1G yields +$0.10). Ram's first-mover proposal can attract both other players because they both prefer green to red; subsequent squid and otter turns then propose 1R->1G which are likely accepted by ram (indifferent between 1G and 1R) resulting in executed trades and a visible pattern: ram accumulating more red while squid/otter accumulate more green.

Holdings have shifted: ram now has more red and fewer green; squid and otter have moved toward more green. Squid and otter continue to prefer 1R->1G swaps; ram remains the most likely acceptor because they're indifferent on a single-chip swap. Ram's proposals to buy red by offering green can still be accepted because sellers (squid/otter) value green relatively highly and may be willing to sell red, but as ram's green stock shrinks his willingness to accept further neutral swaps may decline.

By the final round ram has accumulated substantially more red and fewer green. Squid and otter still gain from selling red for green and will propose 1R->1G again. Ram becomes more likely to decline neutral swaps if they further deplete his scarce greens, so one of ram's late proposals may be declined. We forecast a declined ram proposal followed by additional successful 1R->1G proposals from squid and otter accepted by ram before the game ends.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, three-player bargaining game centers on asymmetric private values for RED chips (GREEN has a common value of $0.50 to all). Camel values RED highly ($1.00) and will systematically trade GREEN for RED; Butterfly values RED moderately ($0.60) and is willing to trade GREEN for RED only when the exchange is sufficiently favorable; Alligator values RED poorly ($0.30) and is strongly inclined to trade RED away for GREEN. proposers request one color and offer another; recipients decide privately based on whether the trade raises their own total value. Strategic dynamics: camel and butterfly will repeatedly try to buy RED by offering GREEN because RED is worth more to them, and alligator is the natural liquid RED supplier who benefits from selling RED for GREEN. Because responses are private and multiple acceptances lead to a random recipient, alligator as proposer will often offer RED in exchange for GREEN (to increase its own value) and expect both others to accept; when camel or butterfly propose to buy RED, alligator is the most likely single acceptor. Uncertainty arises from (a) private values that only each player knows, (b) randomness when multiple acceptors exist, and (c) modest strategic variation driven by players' social priors (camel and butterfly are cooperation-oriented and likely to make reasonably sized, mutually beneficial offers; alligator is resource-protective but will accept clear value-improving trades). Over three rounds, we forecast repeated small one-for-one exchanges of RED for GREEN from alligator to the two buyers, leaving alligator with more GREENS and fewer REDS while camel and butterfly accumulate additional REDs.

rounds.round_explanation: Opening round: proposers will test straightforward 1-for-1 trades. Camel wants RED strongly and will offer GREEN for RED; Butterfly will follow with a similar small buy of RED; Alligator, who prefers GREEN, will propose the reverse (buy GREEN, sell RED). Alligator is the natural seller of RED and likely to accept 1-for-1 deals from the other two, while Camel and Butterfly will decline symmetric 1-for-1 offers to give up GREEN for RED unless the terms overcompensate them.

Middle round: patterns from round 0 solidify. Camel and Butterfly continue making modest 1-for-1 bids for RED; Alligator continues to prefer converting RED to GREEN and will both accept incoming offers and propose its own GREEN purchases. Acceptance remains driven by private valuations: Alligator consistently accepts 1-for-1 offers to give up low-value RED, while Camel and Butterfly decline symmetrical offers to give up GREEN unless they are the buyer. Alligator's proposals attract multiple acceptances, producing random assignment of the trading partner.

Final round: with clearer histories, players repeat the profitable small exchanges. Camel and Butterfly each keep buying single REDs using GREEN, and Alligator continues to offload RED for GREEN. As before, the buyers face each other as potential competitors for available RED, but Alligator's willingness to trade and the risk of random assignment when multiple acceptors exist drive predictable 1-for-1 exchanges to completion.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players trade two chip types (RED and GREEN). GREEN has a common value of $0.50 for everyone; RED is worth $0.30 to owl-blue-3422 and $0.60 to both dolphin-red-8512 and iguana-yellow-5193. That creates opposing incentives: owl prefers to convert RED into GREEN, while dolphin and iguana prefer to acquire RED by giving up GREEN. Proposers must request one color and offer another; responders accept only if the proposed exchange raises their own private chip-value. Given values and the personas, the likely dynamic is: owl repeatedly offers low-value RED to buy GREEN (these trades are mutually attractive to owl and to either dolphin or iguana because recipients who give GREEN and receive RED gain 0.10 or more if they value RED highly), while dolphin uses his turns to buy RED by selling GREEN and typically transacts with owl (owl gains GREEN and thus accepts). Iguana, who also values RED highly, tends to reject offers that would make him give up RED, so his own proposals to buy RED by selling GREEN are usually declined. When multiple responders would benefit from the same offer (e.g., owl buying GREEN for RED), both non-senders will often accept and the actual recipient is resolved randomly. Over three rounds this produces repeated 1-for-1 exchanges where owl's RED falls and GREEN rises, dolphin accumulates RED by selling GREEN on his turns, and iguana gains RED only when randomly chosen as recipient of owl's offers but otherwise fails to trade profitably on his own turns. Uncertainty arises because acceptances by multiple responders lead to random selection of the recipient, and each player only knows their own valuations (so they infer others' likely responses only from holdings and past behavior).

rounds.round_explanation: Start holdings are 10 RED / 10 GREEN each. Owl values GREEN > RED and will try to buy GREEN by offering RED. Dolphin and Iguana both value RED > GREEN and will accept one-for-one trades that increase their own value (they gain 0.10 by giving GREEN for RED). Dolphin will use his turn to buy RED by selling GREEN; owl finds that attractive and will accept. Iguana's own proposals to buy RED (by selling GREEN) are unprofitable for others and therefore likely to be declined.

Holdings after round 0 make owl progressively greener and poorer in RED, dolphin somewhat richer in RED, and iguana holding more RED if he won the random match but still cautious on his own turns. Owl will repeat the same simple 1-for-1 RED->GREEN proposals because they improve his value and will often be attractive to both other players (who value RED more). Dolphin continues to buy RED from owl by offering GREEN and typically finds owl the only accepter. Iguana's buy-RED offers remain unattractive to others and are declined.

Patterns from earlier rounds continue: owl keeps converting RED into GREEN at roughly 1-for-1; dolphin continues to buy RED by selling GREEN and typically trades with owl; iguana rarely succeeds as proposer because other players lose value by giving up RED. Multiple responders still sometimes accept owl's offers, and random selection determines which high-RED player receives the RED when both accept.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three-player repeated chip bargaining where green has a common known value (0.50) and red is privately high (0.80) for whale-orange-8655 and swan-blue-3036 but privately low (0.10) for zebra-pink-5323. Each proposer must offer one color and request the other; responders accept if the proposed exchange increases their own private value. This creates a stable flow: zebra benefits from trading away low-value red for green (gaining 0.40 value per 1-for-1 swap) and therefore will often accept offers that give her green in exchange for red. Whale and swan value red more than green and will accept proposals that give them additional red in exchange for green. Proposers do not control which acceptor is realized when multiple players accept, so offers that appeal to both red-lovers risk a randomized recipient. Uncertainty arises because each player only knows their own red valuation; early trades serve as signals about others' willingness to trade, but the fundamental incentives (zebra selling red, whale/swan buying red) are stable across rounds. Given players' stated tendencies (zebra resource-protective and patient; whale logical and inequality-tolerant; swan intuitive and stable), the likely equilibrium path is repeated 1-for-1 GREEN-for-RED trades where zebra steadily converts red into green, while whale and swan accumulate extra red at the cost of green.

rounds.round_explanation: Initial endowments are symmetric (10R/10G). Early trades will reveal who is willing to give up red. Whale (values red>green) likely proposes to buy red by offering green; zebra (red low) will accept. Zebra as proposer will attempt to sell red for green; both whale and swan (red-lovers) find that attractive and may accept, producing a randomized recipient. Swan then repeats a red-buying proposal, which zebra again accepts. These moves concentrate green with zebra and red with whale/swan.

Holdings are now asymmetric (zebra has more green, whale/swan more red). Players continue the same incentives: whale and swan propose to buy red by offering green; zebra proposes to sell red for green. When zebra proposes, both red-valuing players typically accept, creating randomized assignment of the sold red. The path continues to concentrate green with zebra and red with whale/swan.

By the final round holdings amplify earlier patterns: zebra holds many greens and few reds, making further red-for-green swaps very attractive to her. Whale and swan continue to buy red by offering green; zebra continues to sell red. When zebra sells, both red-lovers accept and the recipient is randomized. The likely final distribution is zebra concentrated in green, whale and swan concentrated in red.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three rounds, nine proposal turns. All players start with 10 RED and 10 GREEN. GREEN is common-value at $0.50; RED values differ by player (turtle $1.00, pig $0.90, elephant $0.60). Proposers request one color and offer a different color; acceptors privately compare the value they would gain against the value they would give. Because all players value RED above a single GREEN, successful trades typically require asymmetric quantities (e.g., offering two GREEN for one RED) so the counterparty does not lose value. This creates a tension: proposers who most want RED (turtle, pig) must give up >1 GREEN per RED to induce acceptance, which can be costly or neutral for them; proposers who have relatively low RED value (elephant) can sell RED in exchange for multiple GREEN and gain value while making the counterparty at least indifferent or slightly better off. Given the personas, turtle (high protection of own resources, stable, cautious) tends to avoid unnecessary losses and will propose conservative yet tradeable deals; elephant (less guarded, generosity/logic) is willing to trade when it increases his own value; pig (structure-seeking, tolerant of inequality) will pursue trades that improve his own chip-value but avoid obvious net losses. Uncertainty arises from (1) not knowing others' private RED valuations, only observing public history and holdings, and (2) random selection of a recipient when multiple accept. Forecast trades therefore emphasize quantity exchanges (often 2 GREEN ↔ 1 RED) that make at least one party strictly better off and at least one party non-worse-off; some proposals are made that are expected to be declined when proposers cannot reach a profitable, acceptable split.

rounds.round_explanation: Early-game testing of feasible price points. Turtle (high RED value) tries to acquire RED by offering 2 GREEN for 1 RED (makes potential acceptors weakly positive). Elephant tests selling RED in exchange for GREEN bundles that strictly help him. Pig experiments with a small request that is unlikely to be accepted given others' valuations.

After observing initial successful 2-for-1 bundles, turtle repeats a similar offer to acquire an extra RED. Elephant again offers to sell RED for a 2-GREEN bundle (profitable for him) and targets the player most likely to accept. Pig, having accumulated some GREEN from prior accepted trades, uses a 2-for-1 seller offer (SELL RED for 2 GREEN) that benefits him and can match a counterparty who is willing to be indifferent.

Late-stage consolidation: turtle repeats the successful 2-GREEN-for-1-RED pattern while holding fewer GREEN; elephant continues selling RED for GREEN when profitable; pig tries one last small request likely to be declined as the others defend holdings. By now holdings are skewed by prior accepted trades, so acceptance patterns favor the player who derives strict gain from the exchange while the counterparty is at least non-worse-off or slightly positive.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players repeatedly negotiate bilateral chip trades over three rounds. Greens are common-value (0.50 each) and red values vary privately: kangaroo values red cheaply (0.10), fox values red highly (0.70), gorilla values red moderately (0.40). Each proposer must request one color and offer a different color; non-senders privately accept or decline. Incentives: kangaroo will generally try to trade away red for green (large personal gain), fox will try to acquire red (since it is his highest-value chip) and may sell green to do so when profitable, and gorilla will favor trades that slightly improve his portfolio value (greens slightly preferred). Because values are private, each recipient evaluates a proposed swap by comparing the value of chips they would give up with the value they would receive; they accept only when it increases their own total value. Strategic dynamics across rounds include: (1) repeated favorable red-for-green exchanges between kangaroo and fox (kangaroo sells red cheaply to fox who values red highly), (2) occasional trades between fox and gorilla when the offered terms make both better off, and (3) some proposals are declined when they would reduce a responder's own value. When multiple responders accept, one is chosen at random as recipient, creating some unpredictability for proposers. The forecast below uses the private valuations and the players' cooperative/exploitation priors to predict plausible proposals and acceptance choices and updates holdings immediately after each accepted trade.

rounds.round_explanation: Early-round incentives: kangaroo, who values green much more than red, will offer small amounts of red to acquire extra green; fox, who highly values red, will accept profitable red-for-green offers. Gorilla is less likely to give up green for red unless the terms are favorable. Expect kangaroo->fox trades and fox opportunistically buying red when terms benefit him.

Mid-game: holdings have shifted (fox accumulated red, kangaroo accumulated green). Kangaroo continues to trade away red for green; fox faces smaller green stock so may propose deals that are marginal and get declined. Gorilla will propose modest-value-improving swaps (swap red for green) that fox may accept if it still benefits him.

Final-round considerations: players mostly pursue individually beneficial trades. Kangaroo keeps converting red to green when terms are acceptable, fox uses remaining greens to purchase red while also occasionally selling small amounts to secure additional red, and gorilla continues small value-improving swaps. Where both responders benefit, multiple accepts can occur and a random recipient is selected.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players repeatedly trade two chip types (RED, GREEN) with private RED valuations and a common GREEN value. GREEN is worth 0.50 to everyone; RED is worth 0.10 to tiger-yellow-3019, 0.80 to unicorn-pink-1559, and 0.50 to bat-purple-4553. Proposers must offer one color and request the other; responders accept only if the trade raises their own private total. Incentives therefore push unicorn to accumulate RED (high private value), tiger to accumulate GREEN (GREEN >> tiger's RED), and bat to be flexible (RED and GREEN equal for bat). Because acceptance is by individual benefit, many mutually profitable trades for the proposer are unattractive to others; proposers tend to pick terms that either (a) exploit partners with lower marginal value for the offered chips or (b) minimize their own loss when they have no profitable options. Uncertainty arises when multiple responders would accept (random recipient selection) and when ties or small net changes make acceptance behaviorally ambiguous (especially for bat, who is often indifferent). The forecast uses these incentives plus the provided personas (tiger: cooperation-oriented but exploitation-cautious; unicorn: reflective and RED-preferring; bat: stable and cooperative) to predict proposals, likely acceptances, and resulting holdings through three rounds.

rounds.round_explanation: Early round: tiger prefers GREEN and will propose GREEN-for-RED exchanges, unicorn tries to buy REDs (high value), and bat looks for symmetric gains or small profitable swaps. Initial holdings are equal, so unicorn's aggressive RED purchases are attractive to players holding RED; bat tries to extract gains by leveraging equal valuation.

After unicorn and tiger have adjusted holdings, proposers seek deals that are both value-improving for themselves and acceptable to at least one counterparty. Tiger (preferring GREEN) designs exchange ratios likely to attract an acceptor; unicorn continues to target RED accumulation; bat exploits opportunities to trade small GREEN amounts for multiple REDs when possible.

Endgame: holdings become lopsided (tiger often holds mostly GREEN, unicorn many REDs, bat many REDs), limiting feasible beneficial trades. Tiger, sometimes lacking RED to sell, may make small loss-minimizing proposals; unicorn continues to prefer acquiring RED when it can; bat faces fewer partners able or willing to trade and may see proposals decline.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players each start with 10 red and 10 green chips. Green chips have a common value of $0.50 to everyone; red chips have player-specific private values: dog (RED $0.50), buffalo (RED $0.90), cat (RED $0.40). Because players know only their own private values, proposals and acceptances are driven by private gains/losses from trades: a responder will accept if the specific exchange increases their own chip-value. Buffalo strongly prefers red (large private value), cat prefers green (green > red for cat), and dog is indifferent between colors. This asymmetry creates repeated opportunities where proposers who offer red in exchange for green can attract buffalo (who gains from receiving red) and proposers who offer green for red can attract cat (who gains from receiving green). Buffalo will propose to buy red using greens when that is profitable to him; cat will propose to buy green using reds. Dog, being cooperative and value-indifferent, tends to propose small, neutral or low-friction swaps that often trade with buffalo who benefits from acquiring reds. Because acceptance is simultaneous and multiple acceptances lead to a random match, proposers often choose one-for-one exchanges (1-for-1) to ensure at least one counterparty benefits and to avoid overpaying. Uncertainty arises from not observing opponents' private red valuations; as the game proceeds, public holdings reveal who is likely to accept (e.g., many reds concentrated on buffalo suggests he is the natural recipient when red is offered). Each accepted trade immediately updates holdings and shifts future bargaining leverage.

rounds.round_explanation: Early in the game players still hold balanced portfolios. Buffalo values red strongly, cat prefers green, and dog is indifferent. Expected behavior: dog may attempt low-friction swaps; buffalo will attempt to buy red by offering greens at favorable terms; cat will try to get greens by offering reds. One-for-one trades are natural focal points to generate mutual gains for at least one counterparty without overpaying.

After round 0, holdings show buffalo accumulating red and losing some greens; cat has more greens. These updated holdings make 1-for-1 proposals attractive: dog can propose neutral 1-for-1 trades that buffalo will accept (profitably for buffalo), and buffalo can continue buying red using green 1-for-1 with cat. Cat will keep selling relatively low-value red to gain green. Expect mostly 1-for-1 accepted trades between buffalo and the other players.

By round 2 buffalo is heavily weighted toward red and low on green; cat holds many greens and few reds; dog remains roughly balanced. The pattern of mutually beneficial 1-for-1 swaps continues: dog can get greens from buffalo at neutral personal value (buffalo benefits), buffalo can buy extra red from cat at positive surplus, and cat can keep turning red into green. Trades remain small and incremental; proposers avoid giving up too many chips at once and favor 1-for-1 increments that are likely to be accepted.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players value GREEN higher than RED (GREEN = $0.50, RED = $0.40), so any swap that gives GREEN to the proposer and gives RED to the recipient is zero-sum: one player's gain is another's loss. Proposers therefore have a clear private incentive to request GREENs and offer REDs in exchange (e.g., a 1-for-1 swap raises the proposer's value by $0.10 and lowers the recipient by $0.10). Recipients have the reverse incentive and will decline proposals that reduce their own chip value. Because every player shares the same private valuations in the analyst prior, rational, self-interested play predicts no mutually beneficial trades: any exchange that helps the proposer hurts the recipient, and exchanges that leave both indifferent require the proposer to give up expected value. Uncertainty arises from (a) small possibility of mistakes or bounded rationality, (b) social preferences or fairness norms that could lead a player to accept a small loss (despite the stated goal of maximizing own value), and (c) random selection if multiple players accept. Given the provided personas (empathic and inequality-tolerant but primarily value-maximizing), the most likely path is that proposers attempt value-improving swaps (request GREEN, offer RED) while recipients decline, resulting in no executed trades across the three rounds. The following forecast uses these incentives and priors to predict each proposal and the expected responses.

rounds.round_explanation: Opening round: proposers will anchor on simple, value-improving offers for themselves (small equal-quantity red→green swaps like 1 RED for 1 GREEN give proposers +$0.10). Recipients, facing a clear loss from such swaps, are expected to decline. Proposers may try modest offers to test acceptance but are unlikely to propose offers that lose to themselves.

Given no trades executed in round 0, proposers may repeat the same profitable-for-them simple offers (1-for-1 swaps) to try to capture surplus. Because every player retains the same endowments and private valuations, recipients again expect net losses and will decline. There is some low-probability chance a proposer experiments with a fairer neutral ratio (e.g., 5 RED for 4 GREEN) to induce acceptance, but such neutral offers remove proposer's expected gain and are therefore less likely under self-interested goals.

Final round: proposers may attempt last-round deviations. A proposer who values social optics (empathy/coordination) might offer a neutral trade (e.g., 5 RED for 4 GREEN) or a slightly generous offer to entice acceptance, but doing so reduces or eliminates their own private gain. Given the strong common-value structure and self-interested goal specified, the forecast remains that recipients will decline exchanges that lower their own value, so most or all proposals remain unexecuted.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players bargain over two chip types (RED, GREEN). GREEN is a common-value chip (0.50 each for everyone); RED is heterogeneous (badger 0.20, peacock 1.00, otter 0.40). Each proposer requests some chips of one color and offers chips of the other color; responders accept only if their own private values make the exchange favorable (or they are indifferent and choose cooperatively). Incentives: badger and otter both prefer GREEN > RED, while peacock strongly prefers RED > GREEN. Thus badger and otter will generally propose to trade away RED to acquire GREEN; peacock will generally propose to trade away GREEN (when she has any) to acquire RED, or sell RED for GREEN only when she values social cooperation more than immediate gain. Uncertainty enters because proposers do not know others' private valuations and must predict who will accept; when multiple responders would accept, the recipient is randomly selected. Personas bias behavior: peacock is the most generous/cooperative and sometimes accepts or proposes trades that are near-indifferent; badger is cooperative but cautious about uncertainty and prefers proposals that give him clear gains; otter is structure-seeking and guarded—she accepts trades that deliver a definite positive net gain. Given these factors, early offers that give clear gains to a single responder are accepted; more aggressive offers that would hurt responders are declined. Over three rounds, expect mutually beneficial exchanges between peacock and the two GREEN-preferrers (badger/otter), with peacock frequently being the pivot who supplies RED when she values social exchange or is offered an even exchange.

rounds.round_explanation: Opening round: all players start with equal holdings. Badger (prefers GREEN) will try to buy GREEN by offering RED; peacock (prefers RED) will attempt to buy RED by offering GREEN; otter (prefers GREEN) will mirror badger's pattern. Early trades are structured so that peacock is the marginal willing acceptor of trades that swap a small amount of GREEN for RED (she values RED highly), while otter rejects trades that cost her too much GREEN for little RED.

With updated holdings, proposers condition offers on who can and will accept. Badger continues to buy GREEN by selling RED and structures offers that are clearly beneficial to a single likely partner (peacock). Peacock, holding more RED, sometimes offers small GREEN in exchange for RED and gets accepts from players who improve their own value. Otter remains selective and accepts only trades that give her positive net value.

Late-stage: holdings are uneven (peacock holds many RED, others hold more GREEN). Some straightforward mutually beneficial trades remain (swapping a little RED for GREEN benefits one side and may be acceptable to the other), but many proposals that would cost responders substantial GREEN for little RED are declined. Peacock's generosity can enable a few last exchanges even when they are slightly unfavorable to her, enabling otter to pick up extra RED when it is strictly beneficial to otter.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This 3-round, 3-player bargaining game pits differing private values for RED chips (buffalo=0.90, camel=0.80, cat=0.70) against a common value for GREEN (0.50 each). All players start with 10 RED and 10 GREEN. Because every player values RED more than GREEN, simple one-for-one exchanges of GREEN for RED would make the proposer (if buying RED with GREEN) better off but would make any seller worse off; conversely, selling RED to get GREEN makes the proposer worse off. Given the rule that proposers trade one color for a different color and each player acts to maximize their own chip-value, mutually beneficial (value-increasing for both parties) pure RED↔GREEN trades are not available under linear valuations. That makes accepted trades unlikely: proposers may experiment with fair-looking proposals (e.g., 1 GREEN for 1 RED) hoping others accept, but rational responders will reject proposals that reduce their own value. Uncertainty remains about whether some responders might accept due to miscalculation or pro-social motives, but the analyst-side prior (players maximize own value) predicts most or all proposals will be declined. Public history is observed between turns, so later proposers could attempt to change terms, but improving a responder's payoff generally requires proposers to offer more GREEN than they gain in RED value, which would harm the proposer and thus is not predicted from value-maximizing agents. Overall forecast: repeated attempts to buy RED with GREEN (small-quantity offers) that fail; holdings remain unchanged throughout.

rounds.round_explanation: Initial round: proposers test simple 1-for-1 or similarly small exchanges to see if any responder is willing to trade away RED. Because responders all privately value RED above GREEN, even fair-looking 1 GREEN for 1 RED offers will be rejected. Observation of rejections signals to later proposers that responders value RED highly, discouraging more aggressive (proposer-negative) offers.

Second round: proposers have observed that simple 1-for-1 offers were rejected. They may try slightly different terms (e.g., increasing the number of GREEN offered) or repeat the 1-for-1 to test for mistakes, but any offer that makes a responder worse off (which includes the majority of GREEN-for-RED terms) will be declined. Because players are patient and value-maximizing, proposers avoid offers that would cost them value and so stick to modest proposals that still fail to entice sellers.

Final round: with two rounds of rejections, proposers understand responders' high private valuations for RED. Proposers face a trade-off: offer substantially more GREEN to make sellers indifferent (which would hurt the proposer) or repeat modest offers and keep holdings unchanged. Given value-maximization, proposers repeat modest offers and responders decline, so the game ends with no executed trades and initial holdings intact.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players repeatedly trade two chip types (GREEN, common-value 0.50; RED, player-specific: Peacock 0.40, Owl 0.30, Dog 0.20). All start with 10 RED and 10 GREEN. Each proposer requests one color and offers another; responders accept only if the trade increases their private total chip value.
