Bear, who values RED and BLUE equally and prefers structure, attempts value-improving but recipient-unattractive swaps involving GREEN (requesting GREEN for RED); peacock pursues the straightforward mutually beneficial swap of BLUE for RED that benefits both peacock and swan; swan experiments with buying BLUE using GREEN but recipients decline. Expect one successful peacock→swan BLUE-for-RED swap and other proposals to be declined.

Middle round: proposers update holdings and continue targeting clear bilateral gains. Bear again proposes swaps involving GREEN that would increase bear's value but require partners to accept a loss; these are declined. Peacock repeats the BLUE-for-RED offer to swan, which continues to be attractive for swan and is accepted again. Swan continues to propose BLUE-for-GREEN swaps that recipients decline.

Final round: peacock again proposes the small, mutually beneficial BLUE-for-RED swap and swan accepts, continuing the pattern of repeated bilateral gains. Swan then opportunistically proposes the reverse (selling RED to buy BLUE) which benefits swan and is also beneficial to peacock; peacock accepts that final swap. Bear's pattern of proposing GREEN-related exchanges continues to be unsuccessful.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, nine-turn bargaining game centers on asymmetric private values for red and blue chips and a common-value green. Players maximize their own chip-value totals given private valuations: monkey and camel strongly prefer blue (high private value), gorilla prefers green (green > red/blue). Strategic dynamics: proposers will try to trade away chips they value less in exchange for chips they value more; recipients will accept only if the chips they receive exceed the value of chips they give up. Uncertainty arises because each player knows only their own valuations and must infer whether others will accept—so proposers often offer trades that are clearly profitable for themselves and plausibly acceptable for at least one other player. Conditional cooperation and risk/uncertainty aversion shape behavior: camel (logical, inequality-tolerant) accepts economically neutral/weakly positive trades; gorilla (uncertainty-averse, stable) accepts clear gains and sometimes neutral trades when they improve predictability; monkey (cooperation-oriented, values blue) actively seeks blue and accepts deals that yield clear personal gains. When multiple recipients accept, a random selection determines execution; when only one accepts, the trade executes immediately and holdings update for subsequent turns, shifting bargaining power across rounds.

rounds.round_explanation: Opening round: proposers test the waters with small, self-beneficial swaps. Gorilla prefers green over red/blue and will try a green-for-red swap; camel looks to convert middling green into reds (and later blue); monkey targets blue but may defer until a favorable exchange appears. Early offers reveal preferences and reallocate chips (notably blue) when accepted, producing path dependence for later proposals.

After initial reallocations, holdings are unbalanced: monkey has more green from camel's accepted trade, increasing its ability to offer red for blue later; camel has more red and begins converting green into blue incrementally; gorilla continues to prefer green and will accept small or neutral swaps that increase green holdings. Proposers exploit known private-value patterns—selling lower-valued chips for higher-valued ones—while recipients accept when the swap yields nonnegative personal gains.

Endgame dynamics: proposers make targeted small trades to consolidate personally preferred chips (blue for monkey and camel; green for gorilla). Because holdings have shifted, some players can safely extract more of their high-value chips. With only one round left, players accept clear personal gains and sometimes neutral trades that secure predictable assets. Random resolution risk when multiple acceptances occur still discourages offers that split surplus too widely.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players begin with symmetric holdings (10 of each color) but different private values for red and blue while green is common-value. Each player acts to raise their own total private-value, so predictable specialization emerges: players who value red more will try to acquire red by offering blue (which they value less), and the player who values blue most will sell red to accumulate blue. Because responses are simultaneous and private, proposers must make offers that create a positive net gain for at least one potential counterparty given that counterparty's private values; recipients accept only when the trade raises their own private-value. Uncertainty is limited (values are known to the analyst) but players only know their own valuations, so proposals also serve as signals about the proposer’s preferences. Over three rounds we forecast repeated, small exchanges of one-for-one or two-for-one swaps that move reds toward the two red-preferring players and blues toward the blue-preferring player, with green largely unused because it has equal value across players and thus offers less exploitable margin.

rounds.round_explanation: Early-stage offers emphasize cheap blue-for-red swaps: Alligator and Tiger both prefer red and thus offer blue to buy red, while Otter prefers blue and is willing to trade her (low-value-to-her) red. Proposers structure deals so at least one other player gains from the exchange given their private valuations; simultaneous responses can lead to contested offers or unique acceptances.

With updated holdings, proposers repeat the profitable pattern. Alligator continues to offer blue for red in small bundles (still affordable), and Otter continues to convert low-value red into higher-value blue. Simultaneous acceptance by multiple recipients occasionally occurs and is resolved by the random selection rule.

Late-stage trades are smaller as some players exhaust the cheaper currency (blue or red). Proposers still target marginal gains: Alligator and Tiger continue to buy red by selling blue when feasible, and Otter continues selling red for blue until her red stock is low. Acceptance patterns remain driven by private gains, yielding mostly unique acceptances but occasional contested offers.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, three-player bargaining game centers on asymmetric private values for red and blue chips and a common-value green chip. Dolphin strongly prefers blue, frog strongly prefers red, and pig values both red and blue below green. That drives repeated, directed offers where each proposer tends to request the chip color they privately value most and offer chips they value least. Proposers will make small, favorable exchange ratios (e.g., trade few low-value chips for more high-value chips) to create deals that are personally profitable and attractive to at least one counterparty. Responders accept only if the immediate exchange raises their own private-value total; because players observe holdings and public history but not others' private valuations, proposers must balance extraction (getting large gains) with making offers that look plausibly profitable to potential recipients. Uncertainty arises from (1) not knowing which of the two responders will accept when both would benefit (accepting players are chosen at random), and (2) imperfect inference about others' exact valuations. Player personas moderate choices: dolphin (structure-seeking, patient, cooperative) will make clear, numerically favorable proposals for blue and often accept mutually beneficial offers; frog (cooperative, socially adaptive) will seek red in repeated offers and accept reasonable deals even if surplus is shared; pig (shorter horizon, less reflective) will opportunistically trade to secure green (the relatively higher common-value chip) while accepting modest, immediate gains. Over three rounds we expect repeated small trades that concentrate blue toward dolphin and red toward frog while pig accumulates green when possible.

rounds.round_explanation: Opening round dynamics: proposers test partners with favorable, simple ratios that highlight their private preferences. Dolphin (first mover) is likely to try to secure blue by offering cheap red; frog follows by trying to accumulate red by offering blue; pig looks for incremental gains toward green by offering blue or red. Responders accept when the immediate value change is positive given their private values.

In round 1 players build on observed history. Dolphin continues to accumulate blue with small, attractive offers; frog again trades blue for red when possible; pig uses surplus red to buy green. Proposers aim for deals that at least one responder privately values, and responders accept if doing so increases their measured chip-value immediately.

Final-round behavior is shaped by holdings accumulated earlier. Dolphin keeps targeting blue, frog continues to top up red (often trading away green or blue when profitable), and pig pursues incremental green acquisitions. With fewer rounds left, players prioritize immediate private-value gains over long-run reciprocity.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, three-player chip-bargaining game centers on asymmetric private valuations for red and blue chips (green is common-value). Each proposer will tend to request the color they privately value most and offer the color they value least. Because proposers cannot choose among multiple acceptors, they often offer just enough to make at least one partner better off; responders accept only when the exchange increases (or at least does not reduce) their own private payoff. Uncertainty enters in two forms: (1) proposers cannot pick the accepting partner if multiple players accept (recipient is random), so proposers may dilute offers to minimize cost; (2) players observe only their own values during play, so they infer others’ incentives from public trade history rather than direct knowledge of values. Given the provided priors and personas, iguana (highly value blue) systematically purchases blue using low-valued red; owl (highly value red) purchases red using low-valued blue; squid (low red value, medium on blue/green) tends to sell red to acquire blue. Forecasted trades are the minimally sized mutually beneficial exchanges that create clear one-sided gains for the intended counterpart, with occasional two-sided acceptances resolved by random selection.

rounds.round_explanation: Start holdings are equal. Early moves establish visible specialization: iguana is a net blue accumulator, owl a net red accumulator, squid initially offloads red into blue. Proposers offer small, efficient exchanges that leave at least one responder strictly better off given their private values; when both responders would benefit, the recipient is chosen at random.

Holdings now show iguana and squid with added blue and owl with added red. Players continue to propose small, mutually profitable swaps: iguana keeps buying blue using red; owl continues buying red using blue; squid sells red to pick up blue. When a proposal is attractive to both non-senders, both accept and the recipient is randomly realized.

Final round: distributions are skewed (owl is red-rich, iguana and squid blue-rich). Players make simple, low-cost proposals to incrementally improve their own portfolios. Responders accept only if their private valuation differential is positive; when both would accept, random selection decides the partner.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, nine-turn bargaining game will be dominated by repeated small, bilateral exchanges that exploit differences in private valuations of red and blue chips while green chips act as a common-value anchor. Gorilla (low blue value, mid red value) will prefer to rid herself of blue and acquire red or green; Llama and Whale (both high blue value, higher than red) will prefer accumulating blue, often willing to give red or green in exchange. Because offers must exchange one color for another and acceptances are private and simultaneous, many profitable swaps (from each party's own perspective) are available: gorilla can profitably sell blue for red/green, and llama/whale can profitably sell red/green for blue. Uncertainty comes from two sources: (1) proposers cannot pick among multiple acceptors and may face random selection when both non-senders accept, and (2) players observe only public holdings and history, not others' private valuations — so they infer willingness to accept from past trades and personas. Given the personas, Whale is relatively generous and likely to accept mutually beneficial swaps; Llama is stable/short-horizon and will accept straightforward value-improving trades; Gorilla is structure-seeking and risk-averse but will propose safe, clearly profitable 1-for-1 swaps (e.g., give blue for red/green). We therefore forecast a sequence of small 1-for-1 trades converting gorilla's low-value blue into higher-value chips and moving blue chips toward Llama and Whale, with most proposals accepted by the party for whom the swap yields immediate private value gains and occasional simultaneous acceptance when both non-senders benefit.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start (each player: R10,B10,G10) gorilla will try to trade her low-value blue for red. Llama and Whale both value blue highly, so they will accept trades that give them blue in exchange for red. Early trades therefore transfer blue toward Llama/Whale while giving gorilla red; Whale's cooperative tendency makes him slightly more likely to accept simultaneously with Llama when both benefit.

Holdings after round 0 skew toward gorilla holding more red and fewer blue, while Llama and Whale hold additional blue. Gorilla continues converting leftover blue into green (highest common value for her), proposing safe 1-for-1 swaps. Llama continues short-horizon moves to accumulate blue using red. Whale repeats profitable blue accumulation when gorilla will accept giving blue for red.

By the final round gorilla has substantially more red and fewer blue and continues converting remaining blue into green or red with safe 1-for-1 offers. Llama and Whale continue to pursue more blue via selling red; most such offers are accepted only by gorilla (who values red more than blue), so trades execute with gorilla as the recipient when she benefits, while simultaneous acceptance happens when both non-senders gain (e.g., green-for-blue swaps).

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players repeatedly propose one-for-one chip trades across three rounds. Each proposer will tend to offer the chip they value least in exchange for a chip they value more. Because green has the same mid-range value for everyone (0.50), trades that swap low-value chips for green or swap low-value chips for a higher-valued color are most attractive. Tiger values BLUE far more than others (BLUE = 1.00) and RED substantially (0.80), so tiger will repeatedly try to acquire blue by selling greens. Duck values BLUE the least (0.20) and will repeatedly sell blue to acquire red or green. Lion values GREEN highest (0.50) and will usually sell RED (0.40) or BLUE (0.30) to get GREEN. Non-senders accept only when the trade raises their own private value (they know only their own valuations). That creates a pattern where tiger frequently accepts proposals that trade away RED for BLUE (since tiger gains) and frequently sells GREEN to acquire BLUE or RED when profitable. Duck offers BLUE->RED trades because that strongly benefits duck; lion offers RED->GREEN trades because that benefits lion. When both non-senders would gain, multiple acceptances occur and a recipient is randomly chosen; when only one non-sender gains, that player accepts and the trade executes. Because players are cautious but structure-seeking, proposals are conservative (small 1-for-1 exchanges) and repeated across rounds when profitable opportunities persist.

rounds.round_explanation: Early round: players test profitable one-for-one swaps. Duck (who values BLUE least) will try to trade BLUE for RED. Lion will try to swap RED for GREEN. Tiger will try to sell GREEN to acquire BLUE. Recipients accept if they personally improve their value; tiger’s high BLUE valuation makes him a frequent acceptor when asked to trade RED for BLUE, and tiger also accepts RED-for-GREEN offers because RED > GREEN for him.

Mid game: observed profitable patterns are repeated. Duck continues selling BLUE to get RED. Lion continues offering RED for GREEN because GREEN is his highest value. Tiger continues selling GREEN to acquire BLUE; when both non-senders gain from a green-for-blue offer, one will be randomly chosen as recipient. Trades remain small (1-for-1) and targeted toward each proposer's preferred gain.

Endgame: players exploit remaining profitable one-for-one opportunities. Duck again offers BLUE for RED (profitable to her and often profitable to tiger), lion persists in trading RED for GREEN, and tiger continues to sell GREEN to collect BLUE. With fewer greens remaining for tiger, offers still succeed when they create positive private gains for at least one recipient; when both non-senders benefit, acceptance is split randomly.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three risk- and value-aware players negotiate a sequence of one-for-one or multi-unit chip swaps over three rounds. Each player privately values red and blue differently while green is common-value. Proposers will tend to offer chips they value least and request chips they value more; responders accept only when the trade increases their own private valuation given current holdings. Because proposers cannot choose among multiple acceptors, they often structure offers so at least one other player strictly benefits (raising acceptance probability), and sometimes both benefit (creating random partner selection). Uncertainty comes from private valuations (each player knows only their own values) and from randomization when multiple accept. Given personas and values: bear (high red preference) repeatedly tries to acquire or lock red by offering lower-valued chips (blue or green); goose (inequality-tolerant and less guarded) often converts some blue into red when it benefits her; ram (values blue most and is a strong reasoner) repeatedly trades his lower-valued red or green for blue when profitable and accepts offers that convert his less-preferred chips into more-preferred ones. Expect many trades where ram is the accepting party when red is requested, and many trades where ram is the proposer offering red to acquire blue; occasional multi-accept offers produce random-matching outcomes.

rounds.round_explanation: Opening round: proposers seek quick, clear gains. Bear (first) will try to buy red by offering low-value blue; goose follows with a similar red-for-blue attempt; ram responds by proposing blue-for-red trades that appeal to both others. Because ram highly values blue, he often accepts trades that convert his reds into blues and sometimes both non-senders accept when the swap is mutually beneficial.

Mid game: players adjust to updated holdings. Bear leverages his green stock to purchase additional red from ram (who still holds some red). Goose continues converting modest amounts of blue into red when ram is the counterparty. Ram, with rising blue holdings, sells a small red to pick up an extra blue when both others see personal gains from receiving red.

Endgame: with holdings noticeably shifted (ram holding many blues), proposers make small, efficient swaps to extract remaining favorable trades. Bear continues to pick up red by selling blue when ram still holds red; goose opportunistically trades red for blue with bear; ram, low on red but rich in blue, offers green to buy an extra blue, and both others accept because green > their marginal blue for this distribution.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, three-player chip bargaining game centers on asymmetric private valuations of RED and BLUE chips and a common mid-valued GREEN. Players repeatedly propose one-for-one (or multi-unit) exchanges, trying to trade away chips they value relatively less to obtain ones they value more. Uncertainty arises because proposers see holdings but not others' private values; they therefore craft offers that are likely to make at least one counterparty strictly better off (or weakly better off) given plausible priors. Cat and Duck both place high value on RED (and Cat also values BLUE highly), while Buffalo dislikes BLUE and moderately values RED; all value GREEN at 0.50. Consequently, trades that move BLUE away from Buffalo in exchange for RED (which Buffalo likes more) or that move RED away from Cat/Duck in exchange for BLUE (which those players sometimes like) create the most mutually attractive opportunities. Because acceptances are private and multiple acceptances lead to a random recipient, proposers often propose deals that are attractive to at least one specific other player rather than requiring unanimous consent. Behavioral priors (Buffalo: cooperative and stable, Cat: patient and inequality-tolerant, Duck: empathic and opportunistic) suggest a mix of accepted efficient swaps and several declined marginal proposals, with proposers favoring clear positive-net trades for themselves and offers tailored to likely responders.

rounds.round_explanation: Early bargaining: players test price responsiveness. Cat, who values RED strongly, will attempt an aggressive RED-for-GREEN purchase that is profitable for her and potentially attractive to players who value RED more than GREEN. Buffalo favors predictable, modest profitable swaps (trading away low-value chips for mid/high ones). Duck will try small margin-improving swaps but faces resistance because other players value RED highly.

With one trade completed, holdings are asymmetric and players begin to propose targeted swaps that exploit others' relative preferences. Buffalo is likely to trade away BLUE (very low value to him) to pick up RED; Cat will opportunistically flip small RED holdings into extra BLUE when it benefits her; Duck pursues small efficient swaps (GREEN for BLUE) that make him marginally better off while appealing to Buffalo's distaste for BLUE.

Endgame dynamics: players try to lock in clear gains. Buffalo, having pushed BLUE out of his holdings earlier, can make a larger move to acquire RED by offering multiple BLUEs; Cat continues to convert single RED units into multiple BLUEs when she can secure a partner; Duck makes last attempts at small beneficial exchanges but faces limited matching opportunities. Because the game is finite, proposers focus on deals with at least one likely accepter rather than on broad consensus.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players start with identical chip counts but different private values for red and blue (green is common-value). Expect repeated exploitation of value differences: butterfly and squid value red highly and blue moderately; kangaroo values blue very low and red low, green is middling for all. Proposers will repeatedly offer chips they value relatively less (often green or blue) in exchange for red from players who value red less. Recipients decide privately based on whether the offered bundle increases their own valuation; when both non-senders would gain, both accept and the partner is chosen at random, creating some recipient-side uncertainty. Strategic considerations: proposers aim for trades that are mutually profitable (to at least one counterparty) while minimizing what they give up; kangaroo's low blue value makes it a frequent seller of blue, squid and butterfly often sell green to acquire red. Uncertainty arises from (1) private valuations (each player knows only their own values), (2) the random selection when multiple accept, and (3) how frequently players will accept small marginal gains. Given the player personas, expect stable, numerate proposals (butterfly and squid) and guarded, self-protective behavior from kangaroo that nonetheless trades when clearly profitable.

rounds.round_explanation: Early moves: butterfly (who values red highest) will try to buy red by offering green (cheap to her). Kangaroo, who values red less than green, will accept such offers; squid will decline. Kangaroo will then try to convert low-value blue into red by offering multiple blue for a red; this can be attractive enough for both butterfly and squid to accept, producing random partner selection. Squid will opportunistically swap green for blue to increase their blue holding.

Players update holdings and continue similar trades. Butterfly again targets red by offering green to kangaroo. Kangaroo repeats the pattern of offering multiple blue for a red; both other players typically benefit and accept, producing a random recipient. Squid again exchanges green for red when it is personally profitable and kangaroo (who values red less) accepts.

Final round: proposers make low-complexity, high-certainty swaps that have worked earlier. Butterfly continues to buy red by offering green; kangaroo again uses blue to buy red and both other players usually find the offered bundles attractive; squid repeats profitable green→red swaps. With repeated patterns, marginal gains shrink but players remain willing to accept small positive-value trades.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, three-player bargaining game centers on heterogeneous private values for red and blue chips and a common-value green chip (all players value GREEN = $0.50). Blue is the most contested resource (highest private value for all players, especially frog at $1.00 and fox at $0.90), while red has the largest cross-player dispersion (fox values RED low at $0.40, bat values RED high at $0.80). Proposers can exploit these differences by offering a chip color that the recipient values less in exchange for a color the proposer values more. Because responders act simultaneously and privately, proposers must target deals that make at least one counterparty strictly better off (or indifferent and socially cooperative) to secure acceptance. Uncertainty comes from private valuations (each player knows only their own values) and from randomness when multiple responders accept. Given the player personas (bat and fox are cooperation-oriented and tolerant of unequal splits; frog is patient and structure-seeking), we forecast a sequence where proposers typically ask for high-value blue or red while offering lower-valued chips (GREEN or RED when the recipient values RED less), and most accepted trades are those that make the recipient strictly better off or indifferent while increasing the proposer’s expected private value.

rounds.round_explanation: Early discovery round: proposers test profitable one-for-one swaps that exploit cross-player value differences. Trades that require a counterparty to give up blue are unlikely to be accepted unless the counterparty is indifferent or gains some advantage. Expect initial failed probing offers, then simple value-improving swaps (e.g., fox buys RED by selling GREEN to frog).

Players adjust to observed holdings. Proposers increasingly target BLUE (high private value) by offering RED or GREEN where counterparty is indifferent or benefits. Cooperative tendencies (bat, fox) make neutral or slightly beneficial swaps likely to be accepted, especially when a recipient is indifferent between exchanged colors.

Endgame: proposers push for last-value-improving swaps. With updated holdings, targeting the player who is made better off (or is indifferent) remains the key to securing a partner. Expect more one-for-one exchanges where the proposer acquires high-value BLUE by offering RED or where fox repeatedly converts low-value RED into GREEN.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players start symmetric holdings (10 of each color) but differ in private values for red and blue; green is common-value at 0.50. Each proposer will tend to offer chips they value relatively less and request chips they value relatively more. Parrot and turtle both value blue highly (1.00) while dog values blue least (0.30), so most trades will consist of dog offering blue to acquire red or green, and parrot often trying to convert red into blue. Responders accept when the immediate exchange raises their own private-value total; because many proposed exchanges are Pareto-improving for the proposer and at least one responder, multiple acceptances occur sometimes, with a random accepted partner chosen. Uncertainty arises from (1) private valuations (players know only their own values), (2) the random selection among multiple acceptors, and (3) limited future rounds (only three rounds) which reduce the value of delayed coordination. Personas bias behavior modestly: dog and parrot are socially adaptive and more likely to accept mutually beneficial offers; turtle is more cautious but will accept strictly profitable swaps. Expect many small 1-for-1 swaps (one chip for one chip) converting low-value blue for higher-value red, with greens used occasionally when they are the lowest-cost offer for the proposer.

rounds.round_explanation: Early round: everyone has plenty of each chip so proposers can safely offer small quantities. Dog will try to trade away low-value blue for red/green. Parrot will target blue (high value) by offering red or green. Turtle will also seek blue when profitable. Responders accept when the exchange improves their own held-value, producing multiple-accept scenarios when both responders benefit.

Mid game: holdings have shifted (dog has accumulated reds and greens; parrot and turtle hold more blue). Proposers continue profitable one-for-one swaps. Parrot repeatedly tries to convert red into blue; turtle continues to seek blue but declines trades that would leave her worse off. Dog, tolerant of inequality and responsive to gains, accepts many mutually beneficial offers.

Final round: players have fewer low-value blue chips (dog in particular has reduced blue holdings) but incentives to convert remaining low-value blue into higher-value red persist. Because only three rounds remain, proposers prefer immediate sure gains. Many proposals remain 1-for-1 blue↔red swaps; responders accept whenever the exchange improves their private value, producing predictable acceptance patterns (dog often accepts when receiving red for giving blue; parrot/turtle accept only when it raises their own value).

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, three-player chip-trading game is driven by each player privately valuing red and blue differently while green has a common known value (0.50). That creates predictable demand: badger and unicorn strongly prefer blue (0.90 and 1.00 respectively) and peacock strongly prefers red (1.00). Peacock treats blue as nearly worthless (0.10), so he is a willing counterparty to swap blue for red. Proposers request one color and offer another; acceptors evaluate the swap using only their own private values. Because acceptances are simultaneous and, if multiple accept, the recipient is chosen at random, proposers implicitly target trades that at least one other will find clearly profitable (often asking others to give up their low-value chips in exchange for high-value chips). Uncertainty arises from (1) not knowing other players' exact private valuations during play, (2) the random selection when multiple acceptors exist, and (3) the risk a trade will be declined if no opponent finds it favorable. Given these incentives and the provided private values and personas, the likely dynamics are repeated, small one-for-one exchanges where peacock accumulates red by offering blue, while badger and unicorn repeatedly trade their lower-value red for the high-value blue they prefer when they can find a willing counterparty (usually peacock).

rounds.round_explanation: Round 0 establishes trading patterns. Badger opens by trying to convert a red (0.60 to him) into a blue (0.90), which is attractive to peacock (blue is 0.10 for him) and unattractive to unicorn (blue is 1.00 to him). Peacock then tries to accumulate red by offering blue, which both others find appealing; a random acceptance determines the partner. Unicorn closes the round by again offering red for blue, which peacock (with very low blue value) accepts.

Players continue the pattern: badger repeatedly tries to turn red into blue (profitable for him), and peacock repeatedly offers blue to acquire red (highly profitable to peacock). When peacock offers to buy red with blue, both other players often find it attractive (badger and unicorn value the incoming blue more than the red they give), producing multiple acceptances and a random recipient. Unicorn again targets blue by selling his low-value red; peacock's low valuation of blue means he typically accepts these offers.

By round 2 the players have clear roles: peacock accumulates red by reliably trading away blue, while badger and unicorn convert lower-value red into blue when they can find peacock. Proposals that ask peacock to give up blue in exchange for red are almost always accepted by him; proposals that would make unicorn give up blue for red are typically declined by unicorn. Random selection still determines outcomes when both non-senders accept.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players repeatedly propose two-for-one style trades (request some chips, offer some other chips) over three rounds. Each player privately values red and blue differently (green has a common value). Proposers seek exchanges that increase their own total value; responders accept only if receiving the offered chips (and giving up the requested chips) would raise their own value. Uncertainty arises because acceptance is simultaneous and, when multiple players accept, the recipient is chosen at random — so responders consider whether the trade would benefit them if chosen, not whether the proposer specifically targets them. Behavioral priors (dog and mouse are generosity/cooperation oriented; owl is strategic and value-sensitive) make dog and mouse more willing to make small, pro-social proposals and accept modest gains, while owl targets high-blue-value trades and designs offers that appeal to the specific counterparty holdings and valuations. Over three rounds we expect some mutually beneficial small exchanges to execute early (often moving low-valued chips from a holder who discounts them to a holder who values them more), and some later opportunistic offers by owl to be declined if no counterparty both benefits and has sufficient inventory.

rounds.round_explanation: Early round: dog (generous) opens with a small, pro-social offer trading red for green to create value for both sides; mouse follows with a targeted small swap that appeals to owl's high blue value; owl then tries a larger blue-acquisition trade, paying red to a dog who values red more, and secures additional blue.

After initial reallocations, dog still has low-value blue and tries to offload a blue for green to a high-blue-valuing owl; mouse then makes a small blue-for-red swap that dog prefers; owl again purchases blue by paying red and finds dog willing to give some blue (dog still benefits from the red). Trades exploit heterogeneity in blue valuations and local chip inventories.

Final round: dog continues marginal rebalancing (selling remaining blue for green) which appeals to owl; mouse attempts a value-increasing deal that is attractive to both dog and owl (both would benefit if chosen), so both accept leading to a random recipient; owl concludes by proposing a further blue-acquisition but cannot find a counterparty who both benefits and has sufficient blue inventory, so the last proposal fails.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players each start with 10 red, 10 blue, 10 green. Red is the highest-valued chip for all players (Alligator & Buffalo = $1.00, Rabbit = $0.90); blue is intermediate (Alligator & Buffalo = $0.70, Rabbit = $0.60); green is lower and common-valued ($0.50 each). Because every player ranks red above blue above green, simple one-for-one swaps that would increase the proposer's value typically require the recipient to accept a loss, so most value-improving offers are resisted. This environment creates two main pressures: proposers who most want more red will try to make package offers (e.g., trading multiple greens for a red) that make recipients roughly indifferent or slightly better off, and more cooperative/generous players (Buffalo, Rabbit) are the most likely to accept neutral or near-neutral exchanges. Uncertainty stems from private valuations (each player only knows their own values) and from not knowing whether non-senders will accept neutral deals; proposers therefore favor offers that either clearly improve their own payoff (even if others must be generous) or that are close to value-neutral so that cooperative recipients might accept. Given player personas (Alligator: strategic and inequality-tolerant; Buffalo: cooperative/generous; Rabbit: generous and low norm-enforcement), the forecast expects a handful of neutral package trades to execute and many one-for-one proposals to be declined.

rounds.round_explanation: Early bargaining. Alligator (strategic, wants more red) tests the group with a neutral package offer (2 greens for 1 red) that leaves recipients roughly indifferent. Buffalo's cooperativeness makes her the likeliest early acceptor. Subsequent proposals in this round try small one-for-one swaps (buy blue for green) that benefit the proposer but impose a loss on recipients, so they are forecast to be declined.

Midgame: Buffalo, having received extra greens earlier, uses a neutral package (1 red for 2 greens) to rebalance holdings. Neutral packages are appealing to both inequality-tolerant and cooperative players, so this trade is forecast to be accepted (selected recipient: Rabbit). Other attempts in the round to convert greens into higher-value chips via one-for-one offers are forecast to fail because recipients would strictly lose value.

Endgame: Alligator again attempts a neutral package (2 greens for 1 red) to accumulate more red. Cooperative recipients may accept neutral exchanges even late in the game; here Rabbit is the realized partner. Final proposers attempt small value-improving swaps but face the same resistance, so those offers are declined.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players start with equal endowments of red, blue, and green chips but differ in private valuations for red and blue (green is common-value). Because each player seeks to maximize their own total chip value, proposers will generally try to give chips they value relatively little and request chips they value relatively more. This creates a pattern where players with low marginal value for blue (buffalo) are natural sellers of blue, and players with high marginal value for red (goose, buffalo to a lesser extent) are natural buyers of red. Bear's valuations are nearly neutral between red and blue, so bear often acts as a liquidity provider who will accept otherwise-neutral swaps. Uncertainty arises from (a) private valuations (each player knows only their own values), (b) simultaneous acceptance decisions by non-senders (multiple accepts lead to random selection of recipient), and (c) expectations about future rounds. Given the personas (goose: logical, patient, inequality-tolerant; bear: empathic, patient, somewhat intuitive; buffalo: reflective, adaptive), we forecast repeated small exchanges where proposers exploit value asymmetries (e.g., selling blue to acquire red) and many trades succeed because at least one non-sender gains immediate value. Proposers tend to favor one-for-one or slightly larger blue-for-red offers (sometimes 2 blue for 1 red) to induce acceptances when needed. The result is a sequence of mostly bilateral exchanges that increase red holdings for the players who value red more (goose and buffalo), and reallocate low-valued blue chips toward the more blue-tolerant player (bear).

rounds.round_explanation: Early bargaining: proposers test partner willingness. Goose initially requests red in exchange for blue (a natural move since red is most valuable to goose). Bear then proposes a balanced red-for-blue swap that is attractive to blue-preferring recipients, producing an accepted trade. Buffalo subsequently leverages its low blue value to offer 2 blue for 1 red, which is appealing to red-valuing partners and executes.

With public history showing successful blue-for-red deals, proposers repeat similar offers. Goose again requests red for blue and this time finds bear willing to accept (bear's valuations are neutral so small symmetric swaps pass). Bear then offers red-for-blue, which attracts buffalo as a recipient. Buffalo again uses 2-blue-for-1-red to buy red when profitable.

Final round: patterns repeat as players have learned one another's incentives. Goose continues to target red via blue offers and finds bear willing to accept. Bear alternates roles and again facilitates blue accumulation by proposing red-for-blue, which buffalo accepts. Buffalo finishes by offering 2 blue for 1 red — a small premium for red that induces acceptance and consolidates red holdings.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players enter a short, three-round alternating-proposer bargaining game where each turn a proposer asks for chips of one color and offers chips of another color. All players start with identical holdings (10 of each chip) but have different private valuations for red and blue chips; green chips are common-value. The dominant trading pressure is toward acquiring red chips (generally the highest-valued across players) by disposing of low-value blue or, when necessary, green. Proposers therefore favor trades that convert their own low-value holdings into red. Non-senders decide only on their private payoff change from giving the requested chip and receiving the offered chip; because proposers cannot pick the recipient when multiple accept, proposers tend to make straightforward, small-unit offers that are profitable to themselves and potentially weakly attractive or at least non-loss-making to at least one counterparty. Uncertainty arises because each player knows only their own chip values; players rely on public value ranges and on observed history to infer acceptance likelihoods. Cooperation-oriented players (cat and otter) are more likely to accept marginal or weakly-improving trades for themselves or indifferent trades, whereas peacock (inequality-tolerant, structure-seeking) accepts only clear personal gains or perhaps neutral trades sparingly. Over the three rounds we therefore expect repeated attempts to swap blue (or occasionally green) into red; some will execute when a counterparty is at least indifferent or benefits, and many straightforward blue->red requests will be declined because other players value red more than blue.

rounds.round_explanation: Opening round: proposers test simple, single-chip exchanges aimed at turning blue (or green) into red. Peacock, who values red strongly, opens with a conservative, mutually beneficial-looking offer. Cat tries to acquire more red but faces partners who value red more highly and so declines. Otter, who also favors red and benefits from green->red swaps, offers a green-for-red swap that finds an indifferent/cooperative partner.

Mid game: players update beliefs from the first round (peacock successfully bought red, otter succeeded with a green-for-red swap). Cat starts again but faces partners who still generally prefer holding red; peacock repeats a simple blue->red request but faces decline because giving red is costly to others. Otter again finds a green-for-red partner (cat) who is indifferent or mildly cooperative and accepts.

Final round: players attempt remaining profitable small swaps. Peacock again tests a blue->red exchange but, with others still valuing red more, it is declined. Cat likewise cannot propose a trade that makes others better off without hurting herself, so declines continue. Otter uses the same green-for-red route one more time and finds cat again willing to accept an indifferent or small-improvement trade, closing the game with another executed swap.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Players repeatedly propose two-for-one style swaps (request one color, offer a different color) chasing their own private per-chip values. All three value GREEN at 0.50, while BLUE and RED have heterogeneous private values: squid strongly prefers BLUE over RED, badger values BLUE and RED equally (high), and camel favors BLUE moderately over RED. That creates predictable incentives: squid and camel will try to acquire BLUE by offering RED (which squid especially dislikes and badger values highly), while badger will often use GREEN as a cheap currency to buy more RED (which he values highly) or accept offers that swap BLUE for RED because he gains in his own accounting. Uncertainty is that each proposer does not know the others' private valuations, so proposals that look profitable to the proposer may be accepted or declined depending on which responder sees a private immediate gain; when both non-senders would gain, acceptances can be multiple and the recipient is chosen at random. Given the personas (reasoning-focused, cooperative-but-self-interested), we forecast mostly straightforward, immediate-value-improving proposals: proposers offer chips they value relatively less and request chips they value more; responders accept only when they immediately increase (or at least do not decrease) their own private payoff.

rounds.round_explanation: Early round: everyone starts with even holdings and looks to move low-value inventory into higher-value chips. Squid will try to trade away RED (very low for him) to get BLUE; badger will accept trades that increase his RED holdings; camel will pursue BLUE but will only accept if it doesn't lower his value.

Mid-game: holdings have shifted so badger has accumulated more RED and is willing to trade GREEN for RED; squid continues to target BLUE by selling remaining RED; camel repeats attempts to convert RED into BLUE but often relies on badger accepting because badger values RED strongly.

Late-game: proposers make smaller marginal-improvement trades to fine-tune holdings. Squid continues swapping remaining RED for BLUE; badger leverages GREEN to top up RED holdings, and camel pursues a final small BLUE acquisition. Responders accept only when the immediate exchange raises their private total value.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, three-player chip-trading game pits heterogeneous private valuations against simple bilateral offers. Greens have a common value (0.50) while red and blue values differ across players: ram prefers red most (0.90) then blue (0.70), dog strongly prefers green (0.50) and values blue very little (0.10), and butterfly prefers red (0.70) over blue/green (0.50). Proposers will generally offer chips they value relatively less in exchange for chips they value more; recipients accept only when the swap increases (or at least does not reduce) their private value. Because acceptance is simultaneous and the proposer cannot select among multiple accepting partners, proposals that are attractive to multiple partners risk random assignment. Uncertainty comes from (1) not knowing others' private values (players infer from prior trades and the public history), (2) random resolution when multiple players accept, and (3) tie-breaking/indifference behavior. Given the personas, ram tends to make structured, value-maximizing offers (willing to give low-value greens to gain reds), dog is socially adaptive but value-sensitive (often willing to trade low-value chips when it improves her holding), and butterfly is a logical maximizer preferring straightforward profitable exchanges. Across rounds we expect repeated small, favorable one-for-one or two-for-one swaps where proposers exploit differences in marginal chip values and opponents accept when the deal improves their own total value.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start all players hold 10 of each color. Ram, who values red most and green least, is likely to try trading green for red early. Dog, who values green most, will respond when a trade offers her green at favorable terms. Butterfly will accept when a proposed swap increases his private value (e.g., receiving green for giving red is neutral/negative for him but receiving red for giving green is positive). Early proposals reveal privately preferred directions and begin to shift holdings, which then change the attractiveness of subsequent offers within the round.

Holdings after round 0 already tilt ram toward more red/blue and dog toward more green. Ram continues to prefer acquiring red by giving away green (green is his weakest), which becomes attractive to dog (who values green highly). Dog, now richer in green, may trade small amounts of cheap blue to acquire extra red when she can make an exchange that is net-neutral or slightly positive for potential recipients. Butterfly selectively offers exchanges to convert green into red when recipients (usually dog) benefit.

By the final round, holdings and prior trades make some one-for-one green-for-red swaps repeatedly attractive to ram and dog (ram continues to exchange green for red; dog often accepts when it improves her green holdings). Dog may also occasionally sell multiple cheap blues to buy a red when both other players would find receiving blues profitable, which can result in random partner selection. Butterfly continues to selectively trade green for red when doing so strictly improves his payoff and when a consenting recipient exists.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players start with equal counts of red, blue, and green chips but different private values for red and blue (green is common-value). Lion values green best (0.50) and red/blue poorly (0.20), while Iguana and Alligator value blue highly (0.70) and view green at 0.50 with red intermediate. Given these private valuations, bargaining dynamics are driven by two currency patterns: (1) Lion will try to trade away low-value chips (red) to acquire green, offering terms that are attractive to players who value red relatively more; (2) Iguana and Alligator will try to acquire blue by offering chips they value less (red or green). Because players only know their own values, proposals are simple direct exchanges (one color requested, one offered) that are accepted whenever the deal increases a responder's own chip-value. This creates many small, bilateral-improving trades: Lion tends to buy green by selling red; Iguana tends to buy blue by selling red; Alligator tends to buy blue by selling green (since green is less valuable to Alligator than blue). When a proposal would benefit multiple responders, multiple acceptances can occur and the realized recipient is chosen at random. Over three rounds we expect repeated patterns: Lion accumulates extra green by selling red; Iguana and Alligator accumulate extra blue by spending red or green. Uncertainty comes from (a) responders not knowing others' private values so acceptance is decided only on their own value change, and (b) random selection when multiple responders accept, which limits proposers' control over who benefits.

rounds.round_explanation: Starting from equal endowments, Lion has the strongest incentive to acquire green and will offer red in exchange. Iguana and Alligator both value red enough that a 2-for-1 red->green offer looks profitable to them; Iguana and Alligator both accept such favorable offers, producing occasional random recipient selection. Iguana then targets blue by offering red-for-blue swaps that are most attractive to Lion (who is indifferent on blue vs red), and Alligator targets blue by offering green-for-blue swaps which appeal to Lion because Lion values green highly.

After initial trades Lion has more green and fewer red, while Iguana and Alligator have accumulated some red or blue. The same incentives drive a repeat of the pattern: Lion again offers red for green (a trade that is beneficial to both Iguana and Alligator), Iguana again offers red-for-blue (which Lion may accept at near-indifferent terms), and Alligator again offers green-for-blue (appealing to Lion). These trades continue to reallocate low-value chips from each proposer to players for whom those chips are moderately valuable.

By the final round the roles are well established: Lion continues to prefer more green and trade away red; Iguana continues to seek blue using red as currency; Alligator continues to buy blue and spend green. Responders accept when individually profitable (or neutral for Lion, who is cooperative and tolerant), and when both non-senders would profit a random selection determines the actual recipient.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, three-player chip-trading game pits private valuations for red, blue, and a common-value green chip against limited-trade opportunities. Greens are uniformly valuable (0.50 each), red is most valuable to whale (0.70) and moderately valuable to dolphin (0.50) and low-value to duck (0.20). Blue is low-value to all (0.20–0.30). Proposers can trade one color for another; because each player only knows their own values, proposals are chosen to increase the proposer's expected value while anticipating which partners will gain. Expect proposers to try converting low-value chips (blue or red for duck) into greens or to accumulate red if they value it (whale). Acceptances follow simple private gains: players accept trades that raise their own value (or are roughly neutral and compatible with cooperative, inequality-tolerant personas). Uncertainty arises because proposers cannot pick which acceptor will be realized when multiple accept; recipients act privately, so proposers rely on probabilistic expectations of who will accept. Over three rounds we expect a mix of declined high-ask offers and several 1-for-1 exchanges where the proposer converts low-value holdings into higher-value ones, often with duck serving as a willing counterparty when indifferent or gaining small amounts.

rounds.round_explanation: Early round: proposers attempt value-improving conversions. Dolphin prefers to turn blue into higher-value chips but faces unlikely acceptance; whale aggressively targets red (highest private value) by offering blue; duck accepts neutral-or-slightly-positive swaps when they benefit. Expect some declined aggressive offers and a couple of small 1-for-1 trades that align private gains.

Middle round: updated holdings make further red accumulation attractive to whale, while dolphin continues to try converting blue into red (positive for dolphin). Duck remains a frequent trading partner because many trades are neutral-or-mildly-positive for duck. Proposers calibrate asks to the pool of potential acceptors; trades that are neutral for one partner but positive for the proposer can still succeed given cooperation-oriented personas.

Final round: players convert remaining low-value chips into high-value ones if possible. Whale continues to accumulate red, often accepting or executing red-for-green swaps. Dolphin, with modest time horizon and cooperation orientation, makes small conversion attempts that are sometimes accepted by whale. Duck prefers to convert remaining reds into greens. Endgame uncertainty (who will be selected when multiple accept) matters less because trades are mostly local value improvements.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, three-player chip bargaining game centers on asymmetric private values: zebra values RED most (0.80), turtle values BLUE most (0.70), and gorilla prefers GREEN (0.50) over BLUE (0.40) and RED (0.20). GREEN is a common-value medium (0.50) for everyone. Because each player cares only about their own total chip value, proposals will attempt to exploit mutually beneficial swaps: sellers who own low-valued chips can exchange them for others' high-valued chips. Proposers will typically offer chips they value less and request chips they value more; recipients will accept only when the offered-for-requested exchange increases their own value. Uncertainty comes from each player not observing others' private valuations and from the random resolution when multiple players accept. Given the personas, zebra (generous, cooperation-forward) tends to make straightforward, predictable offers; gorilla (stable, cooperative) makes modest, utility-improving proposals; turtle (logical, socially adaptive) targets high-value blue and designs offers to secure a specific partner. Over three rounds we forecast targeted, small-quantity trades that convert low-value holdings into higher-value ones while often designing proposals so only one other player gains from accepting (to avoid random allocation).

rounds.round_explanation: Early-round discovery and simple mutually beneficial swaps. Zebra will try to convert low-value BLUE into high-value RED; gorilla will pursue GREEN by selling low-valued RED; turtle is likely to seek BLUE but may reject off-path offers until a clearly profitable partner appears.

With some private information revealed by accepted trades, players target partners who gain from the swap. Zebra repeats a profitable BLUE->RED swap that attracts a partner; gorilla trades BLUE for GREEN when that is attractive to turtle; turtle then designs a slightly richer offer (giving more RED) to secure a single accepting partner (zebra) and convert low-value RED into high-value BLUE.

Endgame trades focus on small, sure-value improvements. Zebra trades BLUE for GREEN with turtle (both gain), gorilla again buys GREEN by selling RED to zebra, and turtle finishes by swapping GREEN for BLUE; some proposals are intentionally targeted so only one other player benefits, reducing competition for the same partner.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players hold identical counts of three chip colors but value those colors differently. Green is common-value (0.50 for everyone), while red and blue have heterogeneous private values (cat: blue highest; iguana: red highest; zebra: blue highest). Over three rounds with one proposal per player each round, proposers will typically offer chips they personally value less and request chips they value more. Responders decide based only on their own valuations and the public history; they accept when the trade raises their own total value. This creates predictable trading patterns: iguana often offers blue (her least-preferred) to acquire red, zebra and cat compete for blue, and green acts as a medium-value transfer that can grease exchanges when direct red-for-blue swaps are unattractive to recipients. Multiple acceptances lead to random partner selection, so proposers often craft offers that are attractive to at least one specific opponent (e.g., offering green to zebra to buy red, or offering blue to iguana to buy red). Uncertainty remains about which opponent will accept when offers appeal to more than one, and in some late-stage situations no profitable bilateral trades exist and proposals are declined.

rounds.round_explanation: Early round: players convert chips they value little into chips they value more. Cat tries to trade greens for blues (cat and iguana have opposite valuations for blue), iguana tries to turn cheap blue into high-value red, and zebra responds by trading reds when blue offers are attractive. Expect straightforward mutually beneficial swaps and a mix of single and uniquely accepted offers.

Middle round: holdings have shifted so some players accumulate their preferred chips. Proposers try smaller, targeted swaps to capture leftover favorable opportunities (e.g., offering a little blue to get red, or offering red/green pairs to strip others' advantages). When offers appeal to both non-sendors, acceptances can be multiple and a random recipient is realized.

Final round: many players hold more of their preferred chips and have exhausted easy profitable trades. Proposals focus on minor reallocations (greasing trades with green or single-chip swaps). Some offers are declined because no non-sender can profitably exchange, while other small mutually beneficial swaps still execute (often between a player with spare low-value chips and a player who values them more).

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players repeatedly trade single-color bundles under private, heterogeneous valuations. Greens are common-value (0.50) so they mainly serve as convenient change; red and blue have asymmetric private values that drive targeted trades: bear values BLUE >> RED > GREEN, peacock values RED > GREEN > BLUE, and swan values BLUE > (RED = GREEN). Proposers gain by acquiring chips they value more than the ones they give up; they must craft offers that yield positive net payoff for at least one counterparty while (preferably) being unattractive to the other to avoid randomized partner selection. Uncertainty comes from two sources: (1) proposers do not know others' private valuations exactly and thus infer acceptance thresholds from observed behavior and prior tendencies; (2) when both non-senders would accept, the realized recipient is random. Given the personas (behavioral stability, moderate cooperation, and modest norm enforcement), players trade in small, efficient increments: proposers request chips they highly value (blue for bear and swan; red for peacock) and offer chips that are relatively less valuable to the intended recipient. This creates mostly one-acceptor trades, low conflict, and progressive reallocation of high-valued chips to those who value them most. Because each player aims to maximize own value rather than group surplus, trades are structured to be individually improving for both proposer and at least one counterparty.

rounds.round_explanation: Early in the game players probe others' acceptance thresholds. Bear (high blue value) targets peacock by offering red for blue because peacock prefers red to blue while swan prefers blue strongly and will decline. Peacock then targets swan for red by offering green, exploiting swan's indifference between red and green. Swan reciprocates by targeting peacock for blue using red as the offer. These small, targeted exchanges reveal preferences and preserve tradable inventories.

With initial signals observed, proposers repeat the profitable pattern: bear continues to acquire blue from peacock by offering red in small units; peacock continues extracting marginal red from swan by offering green where swan is indifferent; swan again sources blue from peacock. Trades remain targeted to a single accepting partner to avoid randomization.

In the final round players consolidate gains: bear makes a larger targeted request for blue from peacock by offering red; peacock again trades green for marginal red from swan; swan takes one last profitable blue from peacock. Because proposals are calibrated to be unattractive to the non-targeted player, each trade executes cleanly without multiple acceptances.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players start with equal holdings of three chip colors but different private valuations for red and blue (green common-value). Trades are one-for-one color swaps (a buy of one color funded by a sale of another). Incentives: gorilla highly values red, dislikes blue; camel highly values blue and dislikes red; monkey values red and blue similarly high and is the most cooperative. Because holdings are public but valuations are private, proposers craft offers that look attractive to at least one counterparty given visible inventories; responders accept only if the offered bundle increases their own private payoff. Key uncertainties: a proposer cannot control which accepting partner is chosen when multiple accept, and acceptance is private so proposers face strategic risk. Expect frequent swaps where low-valued chips (for the proposer) are offered to buyers who value them more, occasional multi-accept situations leading to random partner selection, and some declined proposals where no non-sender gains net value.

rounds.round_explanation: Early-stage adjustments: players convert colors toward their privately preferred types. Gorilla will try to trade away blue (his weakest) to accumulate red; camel will try to exchange red (his weakest) for blue; monkey, being cooperative, will accept profitable swaps when they improve his value. Because many mutually beneficial transfers exist initially, expect at least one multi-accept randomization and one targeted trade where only a single counterparty benefits.

Holdings have shifted: gorilla has accumulated red and reduced blue, camel holds more blue, monkey has more blue as a result of the randomization. Proposals become more targeted: gorilla continues swapping remaining blue for red (targeting the player who still holds red), camel opportunistically purchases red by paying with surplus blue (risking random partner selection), and monkey makes small profitable swaps when a counterparty can benefit.

Fewer profitable unilateral improvements remain. Gorilla still has some green and may swap it for remaining red, camel fine-tunes blue/green holdings, and monkey searches for marginal gains but faces few willing counterparties; expect some single-accept targeted trades and at least one declined proposal when no non-sender gains.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players start with equal endowments of red, green, and blue chips but have different private valuations for red and blue (green is common-value at $0.50). Because each proposer must request one color and give another, profitable trades tend to move low-value chips from a player who values them least to a player who values them more, with the proposer capturing part of the surplus. Across three rounds, players will pursue marginal, low-friction exchanges (single-chip swaps) that improve their own total value. The high-value-for-blue players (otter and tiger) resist giving up blue unless compensated generously, so most feasible mutually beneficial trades involve swapping green and red: green is worth more than red for some players (e.g., alligator and otter), while red is worth more than green for tiger. Given the personas (alligator and otter are reflective and cooperative; tiger is short-horizon but responsive to immediate gains), the repeated pattern forecasted is that both alligator and otter will propose to buy green using red (small positive gain for the proposer) and tiger will often accept because tiger values red more than green. Tiger, when proposing, will buy red by selling green; that trade is appealing to both alligator and otter (they value green more than red), so multiple acceptances are likely and the recipient is then selected at random. Uncertainty stems from simultaneous private accept/decline decisions and the random tie-breaking when multiple players accept; this can shift who receives occasional windfalls, but the overall dynamic pushes reds toward the highest-red-value player (tiger) and greens toward those who value them relatively more (alligator and otter).

rounds.round_explanation: Opening round: all players begin balanced. Early, small beneficial swaps are available by exchanging low-valued red for higher-valued green (from the perspective of proposers like alligator/otter). Tiger values red substantially more than green and so is a natural accepting partner for those offers. Tiger's own proposal will typically buy additional red by offering green, producing positive surplus for all potential partners.

Patterns reinforce in round 1: alligator and otter again seek to convert red into green (small positive gains), and tiger continues to buy red by offering green. Because both non-senders sometimes benefit from selling red for green, tiger's proposals can draw multiple acceptances; when that happens a random draw determines which partner actually transacts.

Final round: with past trades visible, players continue costless, one-for-one swaps that were previously mutually beneficial. Tiger has accumulated several extra red chips and relatively fewer greens; alligator and otter have more greens. The same red-for-green cycle continues, concentrating red with tiger and greens with the other two players.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, three-player bargaining sequence is driven by asymmetric private values for red and blue chips and a common value for green chips (all players value green at $0.50). Frog values red very highly (RED=$0.90) and blue relatively highly (BLUE=$0.50); dolphin only values green (GREEN=$0.50; RED/BLUE=$0.10) and is cooperative and patient; pig has intermediate values (GREEN=$0.50, RED=$0.40, BLUE=$0.30) and is more short-horizon. Because proposers cannot choose the partner when multiple players accept, proposers will tend to craft offers that are attractive to a single likely responder (targeting the player for whom the offered chip is relatively valuable compared with the chip they would give up). This generates repeated one-for-one or slightly levered exchanges (e.g., offering a low-valued red/blue to buy green from a high-green-valuing recipient, or offering green to buy red from frog). Uncertainty comes from private valuations (players only know their own values) and the random resolution if multiple accept; however, given the large value gap between frog's red and others' red, and dolphin's unique preference for green, trades are predictable: frog is the frequent seller of green (in exchange for red) and frequent buyer/collector of red; dolphin consistently seeks green and sells red or blue at low personal cost; pig opportunistically trades with frog when terms are favorable. Each player acts to maximize own chip-value gain per trade rather than group surplus, so proposals are parsimonious and targeted to secure net-positive exchanges for the proposer while offering the minimum compensation that will induce a single acceptor.

rounds.round_explanation: Opening round: Dolphin, who highly prefers green, opens by offering her low-value red in exchange for green. Frog (who values red much more than green) is the natural seller of green and accepts; pig declines because red is relatively valuable to him. Frog then aggressively acquires more red by offering a small amount of green to dolphin, who happily sells red for green. Pig finishes the round by buying a green from frog using red—targeting frog because frog gains from additional red.

With holdings updated, dolphin continues to harvest green by offering cheap red; frog continues to exploit red preference to accumulate more red by offering small amounts of green to dolphin; pig again targets frog when buying green with red because frog benefits from extra red. Proposers repeatedly craft offers that are profitable for one specific counterparty (usually frog or dolphin) so trades execute without multiple acceptances.

Final round: dolphin continues the same pattern — buying green with cheap red targeted at frog; frog still prefers accumulating red and therefore accepts favorable exchanges (sometimes accepting slightly indifference-generating blue-for-red offers from dolphin). Pig uses red to buy green in a slightly larger bundle from frog, who happily trades green for more red even late in the sequence because red remains much more valuable to frog than green.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three risk- and value-driven players negotiate over three chip colors (red, blue, green) with private valuations. Green has a common public value (0.50) while red and blue payoffs differ by player. Each proposer can only request one color and offer a different color; responders accept if the trade raises their own private value. Anticipated dynamics: players try to convert lower-valued chips into higher-valued ones for themselves (e.g., iguana trades lower-value blue/red to acquire green; owl tends to accumulate red because she values it the most). Because multiple responders can accept, proposers risk being matched at random; responders will accept only when net personal gain is positive. Uncertainty arises from (1) incomplete information about others' private values, (2) random selection among multiple acceptors, and (3) how aggressive proposers set exchange quantities. Given personas, iguana (logical, exploitation-cautious) will propose value-improving, conservative trades; owl (inequality-tolerant, structure-seeking) will propose straightforward profitable swaps for herself and accept clear personal gains; squid (socially adaptive) will attempt mutually beneficial conversions but avoid deals that reduce her value. Forecasted plays emphasize small, targeted swaps that create positive-expected-value opportunities for at least one counterparty.

rounds.round_explanation: Early round: proposers attempt obvious value-arbitrage trades. Iguana seeks green (highest private value) by offering lower-value blue. Owl looks to convert greens into high-value red. Squid probes for red but faces low acceptance because others value red highly.

Mid game: proposers exploit observed holdings. Iguana again targets greens, offering red where both others gain; this attracts multiple acceptances and is resolved by random selection. Owl experiments with small blue-for-green swaps but faces limited acceptance because it rarely benefits both others.

Final round: proposers push slightly larger value swaps to improve final payoffs. Iguana executes a multi-chip red->green conversion that both others find profitable; owl performs one more small red-for-green swap benefiting iguana; squid secures a last red by offering green and is accepted by iguana.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players have heterogeneous private values for red and blue chips (green has a common mid value). Across three rounds each proposer will try to trade away chips they value less to acquire chips they value more. Gorilla (high red value 0.80) consistently prefers acquiring red by offering blue. Llama (high blue value 0.80) prefers acquiring blue by offering red. Whale prefers red over blue but places low value on blue (0.30), so as proposer he often offers blue to buy red. Because acceptance depends on the recipient's private values, most profitable one-for-one red↔blue swaps are acceptable only to the player who values blue strongly (llama) when asked to give red for blue; gorilla rarely accepts giving red for blue (she values red more), and whale rarely benefits from giving red for blue. As a result we expect repeated, small 1-for-1 red-for-blue exchanges where llama frequently acts as the recipient, and several proposals to be declined when no recipient gains. Uncertainty arises from simultaneous responses (if multiple recipients could benefit, the partner is chosen at random) and from private valuations: proposers cannot perfectly predict which non-sender will accept, so they tend to make modest, symmetric offers (1-for-1) that are most likely to create a clear gain for at least one counterparty.

rounds.round_explanation: Opening round: proposers test simple 1-for-1 red↔blue swaps. Gorilla initiates by offering blue to buy red (this benefits llama but not whale). Llama will try to propose the mirror trade but finds no partner who gains, so declines occur. Whale also offers blue to buy red and succeeds with llama again. Expect modest, low-risk offers and multiple declinations when offers hurt both potential recipients.

Mid game: players have observed prior trade patterns. Gorilla repeats a safe 1-for-1 blue→red offer that again benefits llama and is accepted. Llama's symmetric proposal remains unattractive to the other two (they would lose by giving red for blue), so it declines. Whale repeats a blue→red proposal that is accepted by llama (who continues to profit from exchanging red for blue). Expect continued asymmetric flow of red toward gorilla/whale and blue toward llama, with many declined turns when no recipient gains.

End game: actors stick with the low-risk 1-for-1 pattern. Gorilla continues to propose blue→red swaps that llama accepts. Llama's attempts to buy blue by selling red continue to be unattractive to gorilla and whale and are declined. Whale again offers blue for red and llama accepts. By the end, red chips have flowed toward gorilla and whale while blue chips have accumulated with llama; green chips remain untouched because their common mid value makes asymmetric 1-for-1 trades less mutually profitable.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players hold identical starting bundles of 10 red, 10 blue, and 10 green chips but have different private values for red and blue (green is common-value at $0.50). All players maximize their own total chip value. Because red is worth $1.00 to duck and tiger and $0.90 to lion, and blue is worth $1.00 only to duck (lion $0.50, tiger $0.30), trades tend to move high-value reds toward duck and tiger, and blue tends to be used as a low-value currency from tiger or lion to acquire red. Green (common $0.50) serves as a neutral medium to make deals palatable to others. Proposers will offer chips they value relatively less and request chips they value more; responders accept only when a proposed swap increases (or at least does not decrease) their own private total value. Because players observe only their own valuations, proposers sometimes make offers that are appealing to specific partners (e.g., swapping a blue that a partner values low for a red they value highly). Uncertainty arises because proposers cannot direct which accepting partner obtains the trade when multiple accept, and responders do not observe others' valuations; therefore offers are often calibrated to attract at least one partner (by creating a small positive gain for that partner) rather than to maximize group surplus. Player personas affect marginal behavior: duck is stability-seeking and tolerates neutral swaps; lion is structure-seeking and will propose trades that are at least neutral for himself and may exploit partners' low valuations; tiger opportunistically offloads low-value blue to acquire red. The forecast below follows these incentives and the exogenous proposer order across three rounds.

rounds.round_explanation: Opening moves: proposers test which partners will accept exchanges that shift reds toward high-valuers. Duck (who values blue and red equally high) offers small-cost green-for-blue swaps to attract players who value blue least (tiger). Lion tries a straightforward blue-for-red swap that is neutral for duck and costly for tiger, so duck is the only likely acceptor. Tiger pursues red by offering blue, which appeals to duck (who is indifferent between a red and a blue). Expect mostly 1-for-1 swaps, accepted by partners who gain or are indifferent.

Players have observed profitable or neutral swaps. Duck has accumulated extra blue and is willing to do neutral blue-for-red swaps; lion experiments by converting green into blue (a neutral move for him) to attract tiger (who values blue least). Tiger continues to target red by offering blue. Expect at least one green-for-blue acceptance by tiger and further blue-for-red swaps accepted by duck when they are neutral for her.

Final round: players push to consolidate high-value red chips. Duck has repeatedly accepted neutral swaps and is predicted to do so again selectively. Lion repeats the green-for-blue swap to extract some blue from tiger, and tiger makes a last attempt to convert remaining blue into red by offering blue-for-red to duck.
