Expect a mix of declined value-destroying offers and accepted small positive (for receiver) or neutral swaps.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players repeatedly trade single-color bundles over three rounds with proposer-initiated, anonymous accept/decline responses. Incentives are driven by private valuations: bear highly values BLUE (1.00) and dislikes RED (0.20), goose values RED and BLUE equally (0.80) and is relatively tolerant, and ram values BLUE and RED less (0.80 and 0.50). GREEN is common-value (0.50). Proposers will try to exchange chips they value relatively less for chips they value more, but any proposed exchange must also make at least one recipient strictly better off given that recipients know only their own values. This creates frequent opportunities where a trade is mutually profitable (both proposer and at least one recipient gain) but also many offers that fail because potential recipients would lose value. Uncertainty comes from not knowing others' private valuations and from stochastic resolution when multiple recipients accept. Given the personas, expect the player who most wants BLUE (bear) to make aggressive offers trading away low-valued RED, and players with large inventories of a chip (goose with many REDs in this game) to be the natural counterparties. Several early mutually profitable swaps are likely; later rounds show fewer profitable opportunities as holdings shift and low-value chips become scarce.

rounds.round_explanation: Early-round dynamics: initial endowments are symmetric so proposers can create mutually profitable swaps by offering chips that some recipients value more than the chips requested. Bear will attempt to buy BLUE by offering multiple RED (bear's RED value is low), which is attractive to both goose and ram. Goose can leverage an expanded RED stock to buy GREEN from bear cheaply. Ram can convert some BLUE into RED by offering BLUE in exchange for multiple RED from a partner who values BLUE relatively more, producing a tidy set of one-sided gains.

After the early swaps holdings are unbalanced (one player accumulating RED, another BLUE). Bear continues to try buying BLUE by offering RED; large RED holders (goose) are the natural acceptors. Mid-round, small, targeted exchanges can move chips toward players who value them most. As low-value chips become scarce in some hands, some otherwise attractive proposals will fail because the only potential recipients who could accept would be the players who no longer hold the requested color.

Late-round frictions rise: desirable chips have concentrated owners and many straightforward exchanges that would help a proposer make gains require giving up too much value (so recipients decline). Expect more declined proposals as proposers cannot find partners who would strictly benefit. Players may attempt modest adjustments, but most offers fail unless they clearly improve a recipient's position as well as the proposer's.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, three-player bargaining game centers on asymmetric private values for red and blue chips (green is common-value). Blue is the most contested resource: ram values blue very highly (1.00), lion values it moderately (0.60), and octopus values it very little (0.10). All three value green at 0.50; red is intermediate and differs by player (lion 0.50, octopus 0.20, ram 0.30). Given these payoffs and the players' personas, we expect repeated small, utility-positive swaps where proposers try to trade less-valued chips for more-valued chips and recipients accept when they improve (or at least do not reduce) their own private value. Octopus, who dislikes blue, is the most likely frequent seller of blue (accepting small gains by exchanging blue for red/green). Ram, who highly values blue, repeatedly offers low-value red to acquire blue. Lion, cooperative and numerate, will broker small profitable blue acquisitions but avoid giving up blue cheaply. Strategic uncertainty arises because proposers cannot pick among multiple accepting partners; when both other players would gain by accepting, the proposer faces random partner selection. Over repeated turns we predict a pattern: lion buys occasional blue from octopus; octopus systematically trades blue for red (or green) to convert low-value blue into higher-value chips; ram repeatedly purchases blue by offering red. Trades are small (one chip) and nearly all proposed swaps that create positive net value for at least one non-sender will be accepted by at least one recipient, with random resolution when both accept. Behavior is driven by individual value-maximization rather than maximizing group surplus.

rounds.round_explanation: Early round: initial endowments are equal, so immediate profitable bilateral gains are available. Lion will try to acquire blue cheaply (octopus dislikes blue), octopus will try to upgrade his remaining blue into higher-valued red, and ram will attempt to buy blue when he can get it cheaply from octopus.

Mid game: holdings have shifted (octopus has fewer blue, ram has more blue). Players continue with the same logic: lion opportunistically buys blue (targeting octopus), octopus persists in converting blue into red, and ram continues to buy blue when selling red yields him a clear gain.

Final round: scarcity signals appear (octopus has relatively few blue left). Players aim for small, assured gains. Lion again targets octopus's blue; octopus continues trading blue away for red; ram makes a final push to purchase blue where available. With limited blue supply, octopus accepts profitable one-chip exchanges, and lion/ram accept when they receive strictly higher-value chips.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players start with equal chip counts but different private valuations for red and blue (greens are common-value at 0.50). Buffalo strongly prefers red over blue (RED=0.90, BLUE=0.10) and will typically offer low-value blues to obtain reds. Duck strongly prefers blue (BLUE=0.90, RED=0.40) and therefore is the frequent supplier of reds in exchange for blues. Cat has smaller differences between red and blue (RED=0.40, BLUE=0.20) and will opportunistically swap blues for reds when it increases her value. Because players only know their own private values, proposers design offers that they expect at least one counterparty will accept (often targeting duck as the accepting partner). Accept/decline decisions are made by comparing the value gained from received chips to the value lost from given chips; green chips (0.50) are not usually traded because they are worth more to some proposers than the alternative. The proposer cannot pick which accepting partner wins when multiple accept; this randomization and private information create modest strategic risk (proposers sometimes prefer trades likely to be accepted by one clear partner). Over three repeated rounds, expect a sequence of small one-for-one trades (blue for red and vice versa) where duck systematically converts red into blue, buffalo accumulates red, and cat opportunistically increases red holdings when profitable. Uncertainty arises from not knowing others' private valuations and the lottery when multiple accept; nevertheless, predictable value differentials drive consistent exchange patterns.

rounds.round_explanation: Initial holdings are symmetric (10 of each). Buffalo and Cat both prefer red to blue, while Duck prefers blue. Early moves are therefore likely to be 1-for-1 blue-for-red exchanges: proposers offer blue and request red (profitable for proposers who value red more), and Duck—who values blue highly—tends to accept because receiving blue and giving red is beneficial. Trades in this round will begin shifting reds toward buffalo and cat and blues toward duck.

Holdings are now tilted: buffalo and cat have increased red holdings, duck has accumulated blues. Proposers continue to exploit valuation gaps with 1-for-1 blue-for-red swaps. Duck remains the marginal supplier of red and often accepts offers to trade red for blue. With repeated interaction, proposers prefer offers that are likely to be accepted by duck rather than splitting acceptances across both non-senders (to avoid the random selection risk), but 1-for-1 remains the simple equilibrium trade pattern.

By the final round duck is relatively depleted in red and rich in blue; proposers still favor simple 1-for-1 exchanges to exploit valuation differences. Duck continues to offer reds in exchange for blues; when both non-senders would accept, a random selection determines the recipient, but proposers still prefer offers that attract at least one accepting partner. Greens remain largely untouched because their common value (0.50) makes many green-for-red or green-for-blue trades unattractive compared with direct red/blue swaps.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players have complementary private valuations: butterfly values GREEN > RED > BLUE, kangaroo values RED > GREEN > BLUE, and squid values BLUE > GREEN > RED. Because each player most highly values a different color, many one-for-one trades (swap a low-value chip for a high-value chip) are mutually beneficial from the perspective of the two parties involved. Green is a common-value chip (0.50 for all), so trades involving green hinge on whether the counterparty values the offered chip more than 0.50. Players only know their own private values during play and observe only public trade history and holdings, so proposers must form beliefs about which partner will accept a given swap; respondents accept only when the immediate exchange increases their own chip-value. Given the proposer schedule (same ordering each round) and the players' personas (butterfly: patient, structured; kangaroo: resource-protective and cautious; squid: socially adaptive and good at reading partners), we expect proposers to repeatedly offer their personally low-value chips (usually BLUE for butterfly and kangaroo, RED for squid) in exchange for their personally high-value chips (usually GREEN for butterfly, RED for kangaroo, BLUE for squid). Because some proposals are profitable to both non-senders, multiple acceptances can occur and the realized partner will be randomly chosen. Over the three rounds we forecast a sequence of small, 1-for-1 swaps focused on improving each player's holdings of their top-valued color, with squid frequently accepting offers to accumulate BLUE, kangaroo accumulating RED via swaps, and butterfly steadily trading away BLUE to obtain GREEN. Public accept/decline patterns will gradually reveal which players prefer which colors, tightening expectations and making similar proposals repeated and frequently accepted.

rounds.round_explanation: Initial round: all players start with equal endowments. Butterfly (who most prefers GREEN) will try trading her low-value BLUE for GREEN; kangaroo will try to acquire RED by offering BLUE; squid will attempt to convert RED into BLUE. Because squid values BLUE highly, butterfly's BLUE-for-GREEN proposals and kangaroo's BLUE-for-RED proposals are attractive to squid; squid's RED-for-BLUE proposals are attractive to both butterfly and kangaroo. Expect immediate, small-volume swaps (1-for-1) with squid often accepting.

After initial informative trades, players have adjusted holdings. Butterfly repeatedly prefers to trade BLUE for GREEN and will expect squid to accept. Kangaroo continues offering BLUE for RED to attract squid. Squid, holding many BLUE, can repeatedly offer RED for BLUE and obtain BLUE from either partner; such offers tend to be accepted by both non-senders, increasing the chance of a trade but leaving who trades determined by random selection when both accept.

By the final round, patterns are established: butterfly systematically trades away BLUE to get GREEN (since GREEN remains her highest value), kangaroo continues to trade BLUE to collect more RED, and squid keeps trading RED for BLUE to amass BLUE. Repetition makes these proposals predictable and likely to be accepted by the player who benefits (often squid when offered BLUE, and both others when offered RED-for-BLUE), with random selection resolving multiple acceptances.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, three-player chip bargaining game centers on asymmetric private values for red and blue chips and a common known value for green. All players start balanced (10 of each). Because green is common-value ($0.50 for everyone), trades involving green require near-equal compensation and are unlikely unless the proposer offers something of at least equal common value; trades exchanging low-value chips for higher-value chips (from the proposer’s perspective) are the most attractive. Bat (prefers BLUE 0.60 >> GREEN 0.50 >> RED 0.20) will systematically try to convert red into blue. Fox (strong preference for BLUE 1.00, then GREEN 0.50, then RED 0.60) also trades toward blue. Frog (prefers RED 0.70 >> GREEN 0.50 >> BLUE 0.30) wants to accumulate red and is willing to sell blue for red. Because each player only knows their own valuations, acceptance decisions are local: a responder accepts a proposed exchange if it increases their chip-value given their private prices. This creates a stable pattern: buyers of blue will offer red (cheap to bat, somewhat valuable to fox but acceptable if net gain exists), while frog will frequently accept red-for-blue offers (gains red value higher than the blue lost). When a proposer offers blue for red, both other players sometimes accept, creating random allocation when multiple accept. The personas suggest cooperative, stable behavior: frequent mutually beneficial one-for-one exchanges of blue↔red at favorable private gains (about +0.40 value per 1-for-1 swap for those pairs), little willingness to trade away green, and low exploitation—players accept only value-improving swaps. Uncertainty arises from random selection if multiple accept and from incomplete knowledge of others’ private valuations, but the seeable holdings and repeated structure make the above exchange pattern likely across all three rounds.

rounds.round_explanation: Early-round dynamics: proposers aim to move holdings toward their preferred color. Bat and Fox both want more blue; Frog wants more red. Proposals that exchange a low-value chip for a higher-value chip for both sides (e.g., seller gives blue 0.30 to receive red 0.70) will be accepted by the seller. Bat will begin by offering red for blue (cheap for Bat, attractive for Frog). Fox will imitate, and Frog will propose the symmetric trade (blue for red) to increase red, which may be accepted by both other players and be randomly allocated.

Middle-round dynamics: patterns from round 0 reinforce expectations. Bat and Fox continue converting red into blue by offering red for blue—Frog, who values red highly and blue cheaply, remains the primary counterparty accepting these offers. Frog continues to seek red by offering blue for red; because both Bat and Fox still gain from such swaps, multiple acceptances are possible and random allocation can shift which partner benefits.

Late-round dynamics: holdings are now polarized (Bat and Fox holding more blue, Frog holding many red). The same profitable one-for-one exchanges continue; Frog has fewer blues but still prefers acquiring red and will trade blue for red when profitable. Bat and Fox continue buying blue by offering red; Frog sells remaining blues for red. When Frog proposes to buy red by selling blue, both other players again accept, producing a final random allocation.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players repeatedly propose one-for-one chip swaps over three rounds. Each player privately values RED, BLUE, and GREEN differently (GREEN is common-value at $0.50 for everyone). Proposers ask for one color and offer a different color; non-senders accept only if the swap raises their own private value. This creates simple, myopic bargaining incentives: proposers will offer chips that are relatively less valuable to themselves (or plentiful in their holdings) to acquire chips they value more, while recipients accept only when the offered chip is worth at least as much to them as the chip they must give up. Uncertainty enters because acceptance can be stochastic when multiple recipients gain (the realized partner is chosen at random) and because each player only knows their own valuations; observed proposals therefore also serve as signals about others' holdings and preferences. Given the personas and values, we forecast frequent small, mutually beneficial one-for-one swaps, especially trades where players with low BLUE valuation (parrot) sell BLUE to players with high BLUE valuation (dog and turtle), and where BLUE-for-RED swaps are the most common accepted trades. Tactical randomness can arise when both non-senders would benefit, producing random recipient selection.

rounds.round_explanation: Initial exploratory round. Dog and turtle strongly prefer BLUE, parrot values RED most and BLUE least. Expect dog to try buying BLUE by offering a low-value chip to attract parrot (who values BLUE low), parrot to then trade BLUE for RED (since RED > BLUE for parrot), and turtle to try to acquire BLUE from parrot by offering RED. Early trades will mostly be BLUE-for-RED or BLUE-for-GREEN swaps that are mutually beneficial on at least one side.

Holdings have adjusted to favor BLUE-rich players (dog and turtle) and RED-rich parrot. Proposers now make targeted trades: dog continues buying BLUE, often offering GREEN (equally valued to RED for many) to appeal to parrot; parrot persists trading BLUE away for RED; turtle continues to buy BLUE by selling RED. When both non-senders would gain, acceptance is bilateral and the recipient is randomized, so proposers sometimes get any willing partner.

By the final round trades are largely routine: dog and turtle, who value BLUE highly, continue to acquire BLUE by offering RED (or GREEN) to parrot, who has accumulated RED and values BLUE least. Parrot continues compact, structured trades (selling BLUE for RED) to shore up higher-value RED holdings. Acceptance patterns are stable: parrot often accepts beneficial swaps that increase RED, dog accepts swaps that increase BLUE, and turtle accepts trades when they gain BLUE.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, three-player bargaining game will feature repeated small package trades between players who value the three chip colors differently. Greens are common-value and uniformly high (0.50), blues are low (0.10) for everyone, and reds are split: unicorn values red as highly as green (0.50) while badger and peacock value red less (0.30). Proposers will try to improve their own total by exchanging low-value blue quantities for higher-value chips or by arranging value-neutral swaps (multiple blues for one red/green) when direct gains from single-chip exchanges are not feasible. Responders decide only with knowledge of their own private values and current holdings; they will accept trades that increase (or in some cases do not decrease) their own value and may accept neutral trades out of cooperation. Because the proposer cannot choose the accepting partner when multiple accept, proposers tend to frame offers that are attractive enough for at least one likely responder (often by offering several blues to match the value of a sought red/green). Uncertainty stems from not knowing other players' private valuations: proposers infer which partner is likeliest to accept based on past public trades and the general propensity of partners (peacock is highly cooperative, badger is moderately cooperative and structure-seeking, unicorn is tolerant of inequality and may accept neutral swaps). Overall we expect a mix of declined opportunistic offers and several value-neutral or small-value trades executed by cooperative or indifferent partners, resulting in modest reallocation of chips (many swaps of multiples of low-value blues for single higher-value chips).

rounds.round_explanation: Early moves: proposers test which swaps will be accepted. Peacock, who is highly cooperative, is likely to propose a fair, value-balanced multi-blue for one-green swap to attract at least one acceptor; badger will try a small-value-improving offer but is likely to be declined because it costs responders value. Unicorn will exploit a previously accepted offer to make a neutral multi-blue-for-green swap.

With public history available, proposers craft offers knowing who has recently traded and who holds more blues to give up. Badger uses a modest multi-blue-for-red swap that peacock accepts (peacock's cooperative tendency makes neutral or slightly favorable exchanges attractive). Peacock then trades a small red for multiple blues to unicorn, who accepts if it improves his own holding. Unicorn frames a neutral multi-blue-for-red swap later in the round to secure a red without loss; cooperative recipients who view the offered bundle as weakly beneficial accept.

Final-round bargaining focuses on tidy value adjustments and consolidation. Badger targets a green by offering a red at roughly balanced value for unicorn (who is indifferent between red and green), so unicorn accepts. Peacock pursues another small neutral swap (blue bundle for a red) that badger accepts. Unicorn finishes with a neutral multi-blue-for-red swap that peacock accepts, producing modest reallocation but no large net gains across the group.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players repeatedly propose one-for-one-color trades across three rounds. Each proposer requests a positive quantity of one color and offers a (different) positive quantity of another color from their holdings. All players aim to maximize their own private chip-value sum (using their private red/blue values and the common green value). Because blue is worth 1.00 to dog and owl but only 0.60 to mouse, proposals that ask for blue must compensate the recipient with enough lower-valued chips (greens or reds) to make giving up blue worthwhile. Mouse values red very low (0.20), so sellers who gain red often accept exchanges that others would decline. Green is common-value (0.50) and can be used as a flexible medium to structure trades because it has identical valuation across players. Uncertainty arises because each responder knows only their own values and current holdings while observing public trade history; a proposer who offers a trade that yields positive value to at least one particular recipient (and negative value to others) can typically predict which partner will accept, but randomization occurs if multiple responders find the trade profitable. Based on player personas, dog and mouse tend to propose relatively cooperative/fair trades (dog especially generous-forward; mouse behaviorally stable and cooperative), while owl is more instrumentally rational (seeking small own-value gains by trading away lower-valued reds). The forecast below uses these priors and basic value accounting to predict which offers are profitable for which responder and whether the trade is accepted.

rounds.round_explanation: Early-round dynamics: proposers try to convert chips into colors they privately value more while offering colors others value relatively more. Dog tends to make generous, easily-acceptable offers; mouse and owl will accept if the exchange raises their own chip-value. Trades that ask for blue typically need to offer multiple greens or reds to induce acceptance from lower-blue-value players.

Mid-game: players have adjusted holdings. Dog continues proposing cooperative trades that help a likely partner (often mouse). Mouse repeats value-improving trades when feasible (selling low-valued red to obtain more-valued blue). Owl looks for small profitable exchanges that target the player most likely to accept (often mouse, because mouse values red little). Proposers exploit these asymmetric values to find single-acceptor deals.

End-game: proposers attempt last-value-improving exchanges. With fewer rounds remaining, players prefer immediate value increases. Dog again offers green-for-blue swaps appealing to mouse. Some small, near-indifferent proposals are declined when no recipient sees a clear private gain. Owl finishes with a trade that leverages mouse's low red value to secure an extra blue.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three rational but heterogeneous players repeatedly propose one-for-one (or multi-for-one) chip trades over three rounds. Each proposer asks for a positive quantity of one color and offers a positive quantity of a different color; recipients accept if the exchange raises (or does not reduce, given some prosocial tolerance) their private chip-value. Uncertainty stems from private valuations (players observe only their own values) and from the random selection if multiple partners accept. Alligator prefers RED slightly above BLUE/GREEN (R=0.60 vs B=G=0.50) and will try to acquire RED from the lower-valuing Buffalo (R=0.50) at attractive exchange rates. Buffalo is cooperative and often accepts near-indifferent trades, and tends to convert low-value BLUE (0.20) into higher-value GREEN/RED. Rabbit values RED much more than others (R=1.00) and will pay modest amounts of BLUE (0.30) to acquire RED; Rabbit is more willing to make profitable exchanges that increase their RED holdings. Over rounds we expect repeated small exchanges: Alligator and Rabbit repeatedly try to buy RED from Buffalo (or from each other) using GREEN or multiple BLUEs; Buffalo often accepts near-indifferent swaps or sells low-value BLUE to get GREEN, and some proposals are declined when they would hurt both potential recipients. Because each player maximizes their own chip-value, most executed trades are those where the proposer offers chips they value less (BLUE or GREEN) in return for chips they value more (RED), and recipients accept when they gain value or are indifferent and dispositionally cooperative.

rounds.round_explanation: Opening moves: proposers test profitable conversion opportunities. Alligator targets Buffalo for RED by offering GREEN (Buffalo values RED=0.50 and GREEN=0.50 and is likely to accept a near-indifferent swap). Buffalo then uses surplus BLUE to acquire RED from Alligator with a multi-BLUE offer. Rabbit, who highly values RED, pays BLUE to acquire RED from Alligator where that leaves the recipient better off.

With updated holdings, proposers repeat profitable conversions. Alligator again buys RED from Buffalo by offering GREEN (a deal that is profitable for Alligator and tolerable to Buffalo). Buffalo converts a low-value BLUE into GREEN by offering BLUE for GREEN; Alligator accepts a near-indifferent swap. Rabbit continues to pay BLUE to acquire RED, targeting Alligator who benefits from the exchange.

Final-round adjustments: Alligator again purchases RED from Buffalo using GREEN at a favorable margin. Buffalo attempts a trade that would swap RED for GREEN but both potential recipients would lose value, so that proposal is declined. Rabbit finishes by buying RED from Alligator using BLUE, a transaction that benefits both Rabbit (gains high-value RED) and Alligator (receives BLUE that Alligator values sufficiently).

Proposers aim to improve their own total by offering low-value chips (for them) and requesting higher-value chips; responders accept when the offered bundle increases their own private value. rounds.round_explanation: Opening round: proposers will try obvious value-exploiting swaps. Badger starts by offering his low-value purple for blue; cat then trades low-value red for blue; dog finishes by trading a purple for green. parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players begin with equal chip counts but different private values for red, blue, and purple (green is common-value). Proposers repeatedly try to trade chips they value less for chips they value more, while responders accept only if the offered exchange increases their own private payoff. Uncertainty arises because each player knows only their own valuations and must infer others' likely payoffs from prior actions and observed holdings. rounds.round_explanation: Early-stage discovery: proposers test who will accept what by offering trades that convert their low-value chips into higher-value chips. Because initial holdings are symmetric, proposers aim for trades that create clear gains for themselves and can plausibly benefit at least one other player. Midgame: players have observed profitable swaps and adjust proposals to target likely acceptors. Proposers favor one-for-one exchanges that are inexpensive to give up but yield higher-value chips. Responders become slightly more predictable based on past acceptances.

Endgame: players exploit remaining easy gains and avoid trades that would create shared acceptance (which would risk random assignment). Proposers often repeat successful small trades; responders accept clear personal gains even late in the sequence.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, nine-turn bargaining game is driven by private chip valuations and simple one-for-one exchanges: each proposer requests one color and offers a different color in return. Because green has a common value (0.50) while red/blue/purple values differ by player, trades tend to move chips from players who value them less to players who value them more. Player personas bias likely choices: dog-orange-6132 (cooperative, generous) is somewhat tolerant of even marginally beneficial swaps; buffalo-green-3116 seeks structure and avoids losses so prefers clear gains and favors trading away cheap purple; bear-red-1079 protects own resources but will trade low-value red for higher-value green/purple. rounds.round_explanation: Opening round: all players start with 10 of each color. Proposers look for straightforward, small advantageous swaps. Dog will try to convert a chip she values less (purple) into one she values more (red); buffalo exploits her very low purple valuation to try to acquire green; bear looks to buy green by offering purple to a player who values purple more. Mid game: holdings have shifted (dog has more red/purple, buffalo has more green), so proposers adjust. Dog targets blue by offering green when a partner values blue less than she does (bear). Buffalo continues exploiting low purple by asking for red in exchange for purple. Bear looks for green via offering red; because both others can find such a swap acceptable, multiple acceptances are possible and the realized recipient is randomized.

End game: players focus on guaranteed personal gains since there's no future beyond this round. Dog again swaps a marginally lower-valued purple for a high-valued red; buffalo converts cheap purple into green (clear gain); bear trades low-value red for purple, which both other players find beneficial so there is again a multi-accept chance and then a random recipient is realized.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players repeatedly propose single-color-for-single-color trades over 3 rounds. Each proposer asks for a chip color they privately value more and offers a color they value less (or equal) given their private valuations; responders accept only if the trade increases their own total chip value given their private values. Greens are common-value (0.50) and often used as a neutral currency to bridge value gaps. When multiple responders accept, a random accepting recipient is selected, creating risk for proposers and making offers that create a single clear winner more attractive. rounds.round_explanation: Opening round: proposers test partner preferences. Early offers are modest one-for-one swaps that ask for a chip the proposer values more while offering a chip of neutral or lower private value. Responders accept only when the immediate private-value gain is clear (or indifferent but socially inclined). Expect at least one accepted trade where the recipient strictly benefits.

After an initial successful trade, proposers update expectations about who will accept. Squid, having traded away some blues, is more willing to accept offers that trade blues for high-value purples. Proposers may receive multiple acceptances when offers create gains for multiple players; random selection then determines who transacts.

Final round: players exploit clearer mutual gains and avoid risky or zero-sum offers. Proposers target partners who are likely to gain based on observed holdings and inferred preferences. parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players start with identical holdings but different private values for red/blue/purple; green is common-value. Personas make repeated, clear-value-improving trades likely: otter (structure-seeking, cooperation-oriented) repeatedly seeks high-value red/blue while tolerating unequal outcomes; bear (behaviorally stable) prefers predictable, structure-friendly exchanges converting low-value purple into red/blue; dog (empathetic, highly cooperative) readily accepts trades that increase his own value (especially gaining purple). Observationally, we expect many small 1-for-1 swaps of purple for red/blue exploiting the value asymmetries, with several rounds producing accepted trades and occasional rounds where both non-senders accept, triggering random recipient selection.

rounds.round_explanation: Initial endowments are equal. rounds.round_explanation: Opening round: proposers test the market. Otter then tries to buy GREEN by offering his low-valued BLUEs to attract a single partner (targets cat who values BLUE relatively highly); badger pursues straightforward red-for-green swaps that capitalize on his low valuation of RED.

Proposers target obvious mutually beneficial swaps: lion will try to acquire blue/red by offering low-value purple; butterfly will target purple by offering low-value red; camel will try to convert low-value purple/red into blue. Responders will accept when the item they receive raises their own value.

Mid game: holdings have shifted so some players hold more of their preferred colors (butterfly accumulates purple). Lion continues to offload purple to obtain more red/blue; butterfly opportunistically trades low-value red for medium-value green; camel continues to convert purple into blue. Responders accept based on the private gains available after seeing current public holdings.

Final round: players consolidate desirable colors. Lion again trades away remaining low-value purple to pick up additional blue. Butterfly uses some blue (now scarcer) to secure green (medium-value) when profitable; multiple responders may benefit from some proposals and one is chosen at random. Camel continues to acquire blue by offering low-value purple. End-of-game holdings show lion concentrated in red/blue, butterfly concentrated in purple and some green, and camel concentrated in blue.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This 3-round, 3-player chip-trading game centers on private heterogeneous valuations for red/blue/purple chips and a common-value green chip. Each proposer can only offer one color in exchange for one different color; recipients decide using their own private marginal values, so acceptance requires a recipient-level gain (value(received) - value(given) > = 0) or, sometimes, indifference plus social tendencies. Proposers will only propose deals that increase (or at least not sharply decrease) their own expected payoff given their private values and the likely acceptances by others. Uncertainty arises because each player knows only their own valuations (not others'), so proposers reason from priors about who will accept; recipients face random selection if multiple accept. The dynamic incentives push players to trade away chips they value less in order to gain chips they value more; to secure a trading partner proposers sometimes must offer favorable terms to recipients, but those terms must still be acceptable to the proposer. Given the provided personas, we expect reasonably straightforward, value-improving offers (e.g., using low-value purple/red to obtain green/blue), occasional indifferent acceptances resolved by social tendencies, and several declined proposals when recipients would lose value.

rounds.round_explanation: Early-round search for mutually beneficial conversions: squid tries to acquire high-value blue but asks too little favorable terms and is declined; camel looks to improve her bundle by swapping low-value red for higher-valued purple (a small self-benefit) and finds a willing partner; lion looks to upgrade to blue by offering purple but cannot make the proposal palatable to either other player without losing value, so declines.

Players exploit small value differences: squid converts low-value purple into common green (which she values more), attracting a partner; camel similarly upgrades from red to green by offering a modestly valued red in exchange for green; lion finishes the round by buying an extra red from green (he values red highly and is willing to give green), producing accepted, value-improving swaps for proposers and recipients.

Final-round efficiency moves where proposers trade away lower-valued chips (from their perspective) for higher-valued chips while targeting recipients who benefit. Squid repeats a small purple→green upgrade; camel executes a small purple-for-red swap that is acceptable to lion; lion finishes by exchanging a green for a red (both recipients gain or are indifferent), concluding the game with locally value-improving trades.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players start with identical chip counts but different private values for red/blue/purple (green is common value). Each proposer will try to exchange chips they value relatively less for chips they value more while offering enough quantity to make the deal attractive to at least one recipient. Because accept/reject decisions are private and recipients are chosen at random when multiple accept, proposers face a tradeoff: make offers minimally profitable for themselves (smaller gains) that attract many partners, or make larger-self-gain offers that may be rejected. Uncertainty comes from not knowing others' private valuations; however repeated visible trades provide informative signals. Given the personas, peacock and alligator are more likely to accept mutually beneficial quantity trades, squid is cautious but will accept clear value gains. Expect many small, quantity-adjusted exchanges (e.g., multiple cheap chips for a single high-value chip) rather than 1-for-1 swaps when values diverge.

rounds.round_explanation: Opening round: proposers test the market and signal preferences. Squid (structure-seeking, exploitation-cautious) will try a modest, low-risk ask and is likely to withdraw quickly if no partner accepts. Peacock (cooperation-oriented) will make a quantity trade that creates gains for typical recipients (selling weak red in exchange for high-value blue). Alligator (generous, logical) will aim to convert low-value purple into green by offering purple for green, expecting at least one partner to accept.

After the opening trades, players have evidence about who is willing to trade. Squid, having seen peacock accept favorable quantity exchanges, will make a slightly larger-quantity offer to pick up a blue chip by selling cheap purple chips. Peacock continues converting low-value red into blue where profitable. Alligator repeats a profitable purple-for-blue swap, targeting players who benefit from that quantity exchange.

Final round: players refine positions and convert remaining low-value chips. Squid makes a small, safe trade (purple for red). Peacock again converts red into blue using a quantity premium; because both squid and alligator find the exchange attractive, multiple acceptances can occur and a random selection will determine the recipient. Alligator uses leftover purple to pry off additional green from a cooperating peacock.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players with heterogeneous private values negotiate a sequence of single-color one-for-one chip trades over three rounds. Each proposer tends to offer chips they value relatively little and request chips they value more, while responders accept only when the exchange raises their own total value. Uncertainty enters because each player sees only their own values and holdings, so proposers must craft offers that are plausibly attractive to at least one counterpart given public holdings and typical preferences. Owl (patient, structure-seeking) is willing to make modest, logically favorable exchanges and to accept small gains; Alligator (short-horizon, cooperation-oriented) is willing to accept exchanges that give them high-value chips quickly; Ram (cooperative, intuitive) often accepts clear improvements but is more conservative when indifferent or facing losses. Because red is highly valued by Alligator and Ram, and owl values purple relatively highly while valuing blue poorly, the play concentrates on owl offering blue or other low-valued chips to acquire purple (or occasionally green), and Alligator exploiting low-valued purple to acquire reds. Trades execute when they are mutually improving for the acceptor; if no acceptor gains, the proposal is declined. Over the three rounds we forecast a pattern of repeated small advantageous swaps for the proposer or a receptive counterpart, with a few declined offers when no counterparty has a strict incentive to accept.

rounds.round_explanation: Early-round incentives: owl starts by trying to convert her low-value blue into higher-value purple, which looks attractive to Alligator (who values blue highly and purple little). Alligator then leverages low-value purple to request red from owl. Ram proposes red-for-blue but faces rejection because others value red more highly.

With holdings updated, owl again trades away low-value blue to acquire a green (relatively valuable for her), attracting Alligator who prefers blue. Alligator repeats a purple-for-red request to owl when that exchange is slightly beneficial for owl. Ram's attempt to obtain red by offering blue is rejected because both others would lose value by selling red.

Final-round dynamics show owl continuing to trade her low-valued blue for extra purple (or green) when counterparts benefit. Alligator continues to convert remaining low-value purple into reds by offering purple, which owl accepts when it yields a small gain. Ram times a purple-for-red flip at the end that owl accepts because it is marginally favorable for her.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players each start with 10 of each chip type. Green chips are common-value (0.50 to all); red/blue/purple have different private values. Each proposer will try to improve their own total chip value by offering chips they value relatively less and requesting chips they value relatively more. Because players only observe their own private values, proposals that are attractive to a proposer are targeted at partners who are likely (given the population priors) to value the offered chip more than the requested chip. This creates asymmetric-demand trades: participants often trade away chips they value less (purple for some, blue for others) to obtain chips they value more. Uncertainty arises because proposers cannot direct the recipient; offers are sent to both non-senders and, if multiple accept, one accepting recipient is chosen at random. Given the provided personas, butterfly and cat are analytically driven and willing to accept trades that clearly increase their own total value; badger is more guarded and accepts primarily when the trade gives him a clear benefit. Over three rounds (each with three proposal turns) we expect a sequence of small mutually profitable swaps: frequent trades of purple (high value to badger, low to cat) and blue (high to cat, mid to butterfly, low to badger), with green used occasionally as a medium-value sweetener. Some proposals will be declined when neither non-sender gains value from the exchange.

rounds.round_explanation: Early in the game everyone still has symmetric endowments. Proposers will exploit known cross-player value heterogeneity: cat values purple very little and blue relatively highly, badger values purple highly and blue low, and butterfly values purple and red relatively high. Expect butterfly and badger to try to buy purple from cat by offering blue; cat will often accept. Cat will try to sell purple for blue to accumulate higher-valued blue.

Holdings have become asymmetric: cat has accumulated blue and shed purple, badger has increased purple, butterfly has modestly shifted holdings. Proposers will continue to offer chips they value less (blue for butterfly is a reasonable currency to buy red or purple; badger will keep offering blue to buy purple). Cat will continue to monetize remaining purple by buying blue when profitable.

Late-game dynamics: players have clearer signals from observed trades about who actually parts with which chips. Badger has accumulated purple and will still prefer to trade away his low-valued blue for additional purple when possible. Cat has built a large blue stock and uses green occasionally to top up blue when it yields a private-value gain. Butterfly continues to use blue to buy red or purple. Some offers will be narrowly profitable only for one counterparty, producing clean acceptances.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, three-player bargaining forecast uses the provided private chip valuations and proposer order to predict which simple one-for-one exchanges are proposed and which counterparties accept. Dynamics: proposers try to trade away chips they value relatively little and request chips they value more, but they must anticipate acceptance given only public information (green = common 0.50) and the fact that recipients only know their own valuations. Because green has a common value, it is often used as a low-friction currency to make otherwise-neutral swaps. When a proposed swap strictly increases a non-sender's private value they accept; indifferent swaps may be accepted by cooperation-oriented players (here goose and ram are relatively cooperation-prone). Multiple acceptances cause a random selection of the recipient; proposers cannot pick the partner. Uncertainty stems from private valuations for red/blue/purple (public range 0.10–1.00) and from whether indifferent recipients will accept. Given the players' personas (ram and goose lean cooperative, butterfly is structure-seeking and inequality-tolerant) and their private valuations (butterfly strongly values blue/purple, ram heavily values purple, goose moderately values blue/purple), the forecast emphasizes small 1-for-1 swaps where the proposer trades a lower-valued color for a higher-valued color for themselves, and recipients accept only when their personal value increases (or when indifferent and cooperative).

rounds.round_explanation: Opening round: proposers test simple, low-commitment 1-for-1 swaps. Early offers target purple (high for ram and butterfly) and blue (middling for goose). Because green is common-value, it is a convenient bridging asset but is not yet widely traded in this round. Non-senders accept only when their private value increases; many pure value-improving swaps are directed at the player who benefits most.

Mid game: players react to observed trades. Ram successfully uses green as a neutral bridge to pick up an extra purple via an indifferent-but-cooperative acceptance by goose. Goose leverages extra green to buy blue from ram. Butterfly targets blue by selling low-valued red. These are still small 1-for-1 trades; acceptance follows strict personal-value gains (or cooperative indifference in the green->purple swap).

Final round: proposers push for straightforward value-improving swaps. Ram tries to turn low-value blue into higher-value red and succeeds because both other players would gain from giving up red for blue; multiple acceptances occur and the recipient is chosen at random. Goose repeats a previously successful blue-for-red swap, targeting ram who benefits from the exchange. Butterfly again swaps red for blue to increase her own value. With only one round left, trades remain simple and individually beneficial rather than gambits for larger multi-chip deals.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, nine-turn bargaining game pits three players with different private valuations for red/blue/purple chips and a common valuation for green. Each proposer will try to exchange chips they value less for chips they value more, while recipients decide whether the immediate exchange increases their own total value. Uncertainty arises because players only know their own private values; they infer others' likely preferences from prior offers and observed acceptances. With two cooperative, reasoning-oriented players (iguana and whale) and one highly blue-loving, patient player (duck), we expect many small, mutually beneficial 1-for-1 trades: iguana will trade away low-value red, whale will trade away mid-value blue or purple to acquire red/green, and duck will repeatedly try to acquire blue by offering red or low-value purple. Because proposals can attract multiple acceptors, some trades will be resolved by random selection among acceptors; this adds strategic noise and makes proposers sometimes aim offers that create a single clear beneficiary. Over three rounds the likely pattern is incremental reallocations that move blue chips toward duck and blue/green chips toward those who value them, with frequent accepted 1-for-1 swaps and occasional declined proposals when recipients would be made worse off.

rounds.round_explanation: Opening moves: proposers will seek easy, mutually beneficial 1-for-1 swaps. Iguana (who most strongly values blue) tries to buy blue by offering low-value red; whale (who values red and green) responds opportunistically; duck aims to pick up blue when it can offer something acceptable.

After initial reallocations, players refine trades. Iguana still looks to upgrade low-value red into higher-value chips (green/blue). Whale uses surplus red to acquire more preferred chips or to trade blue away. Duck continues to pursue blue using low-value purple or red as payment; acceptances depend on whether recipients gain or are at least indifferent.

Final round: players squeeze remaining gains. Iguana continues small upgrades (trading away red), whale opportunistically trades blue for green or red, and duck makes a last push to accumulate blue. Recipients continue to accept trades that clearly increase their private value or are small-neutral gains consistent with cooperative tendencies.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players start with equal holdings of four chip colors but different private values for red/blue/purple (green common-valued). Bargaining incentives are simple: each proposer requests one color and offers another; they will propose exchanges that convert chips they value less into chips they value more. Recipients decide only with their private valuations and current holdings, so acceptance depends on whether the offered bundle increases their own total value. Common-value green (0.50) acts as a liquidity/medium-of-exchange that some players are willing to trade away to get higher private-value chips. Uncertainty arises from (1) incomplete information about others' valuations, (2) the possibility of multiple acceptances (recipient chosen at random), and (3) dynamic changes in holdings after each accepted trade which shift later incentives. Given the players' personas, proposals tend to be reasonably efficient and transparent: lion and owl (higher numeracy and strategic reasoning) craft trades that look like strict gains for themselves, while ram (cooperative but exploitation-cautious) makes modest, socially readable offers. We forecast a mix of accepted and declined proposals: some two-way beneficial trades are accepted readily, occasionally both non-senders accept producing a random recipient, and some proposals are declined because they decrease recipients' private value.

rounds.round_explanation: Initial holdings are symmetric (10 of each chip). Early proposals test others' willingness to trade green as a medium and to exchange low-value red for higher-value blue/purple. Expect some straightforward mutually beneficial swaps (e.g., selling green for red/blue) and some exploratory offers that get declined when they hurt recipients.

After round 0 accepted trades, holdings are asymmetric: lion has accumulated more red, owl picked up green/blue, ram unchanged. Proposers now leverage those imbalances: ram trades green for red (both recipients see gains and both accept, producing a randomization); lion trades low-value blue for high-value purple; owl trades low-value red for blue with the player who values blue relatively more accepting.

With further asymmetry (lion holding many reds/purples, ram holding more blue after prior acceptance, owl having boosted blue), proposers exploit surplus positions. Ram swaps lower-valued purple for blue (accepted by lion who has blue to spare), lion trades red for purple targeting ram who values blue/purple relatively highly, and owl's late attempt to reallocate from tiny red holdings is unlikely to be accepted and is therefore declined.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players start with equal nominal holdings but different private valuations for red, blue, and purple (green is common-value). Each player will try to trade away chips they value relatively less for chips they value more; because proposers cannot pick the accepting partner, they tend to offer deals that at least one other player strictly prefers. This creates predictable patterns: offers that give blue to owl (who values blue very highly) require large compensation and therefore are unlikely to be accepted by owl, while offers that give blue to ram (who values blue lowest) are relatively cheap for the proposer to procure. Uncertainty arises from simultaneous private accept/decline by the two non-senders and the random tie-breaking when both accept; proposers therefore tend to propose low-cost trades that reliably attract at least one acceptor rather than gamble on a high-value partner accepting. Behaviorally, ram's cooperation orientation makes him likely to accept mutually beneficial swaps; butterfly's resource-protectiveness makes her favor safe, small profitable trades; owl's short horizon and strong preference for blue make her quick to accept clear gains even if she won't give up her best chips cheaply.

rounds.round_explanation: Early in the game players still hold many of every color. Proposers target straightforward one-for-one exchanges: request a higher-valued chip for oneself while offering a lower-value chip to someone who values the requested chip less. Trades that transfer blue from ram (low blue value) to others are particularly cheap and therefore likely to be executed early.

After the first round holdings shift modestly toward blue for butterfly and owl and toward purple/green for ram. Proposers continue to craft offers that produce clear gains for at least one counterparty (e.g., giving red or blue to ram in exchange for blue or purple), avoiding large-risk offers that would only be attractive to owl.

Late-game moves exploit the established imbalances: butterfly continues to buy blue cheaply from ram, ram continues to acquire purple by offering low-value chips or by buying from butterfly, and owl picks up additional blue when it can be obtained at low cost (usually from ram). The randomness of multi-accept situations still creates some pickup uncertainty, but by now proposers favor trades that reliably attract at least one acceptor.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players start with identical chip endowments but different private values for red/blue/purple (green common-value). Each proposer will try to exchange chips they value less for chips they value more; responders accept if the offered exchange increases their own private total value. Uncertainty arises because each player knows only their own valuations and must infer others' likely gains from a trade using public holdings and past trades. Strategic considerations: proposers tend to offer minimal concessions that still entice at least one partner; players with high valuation for a chip (e.g., whale for red, dolphin for blue) are natural buyers and will pay with chips they value less; when both non-senders would benefit, acceptance by multiple players creates partner-selection risk (proposer cannot choose partner). Over three rounds, earlier accepted trades alter holdings and shift who can profitably trade next; empathetic/adaptive tendencies (owl, whale) make small neutral gains more likely to be accepted by them than by more exploitation-cautious players, producing a mix of one-sided accepted trades and some declined offers when proposed splits are unfavorable to all recipients.

rounds.round_explanation: Early round: everyone starts with 10 of each chip. Dolphin (high blue value) will try to buy blue by giving cheaper purple; Owl (blue-preferring and socially adaptive) will try small beneficial swaps; Whale (high red value) will seek red. Proposers aim for trades that yield modest personal gains while targeting recipients who likely lose less or gain from the swap.

Holdings have shifted: Dolphin has more blue, Whale more purple and red, Owl slightly adjusted. Dolphin continues to target blue by selling purple; Owl chooses a low-cost green-for-red swap to secure a small gain; Whale again seeks red via small, mutually acceptable exchanges. Responders consider realized holdings so that only those who gain will accept.

Final round: players exploit remaining mutually beneficial margins. Dolphin, still valuing blue highly, trades a red for blue and targets Whale who can spare blue. Owl uses a small purple-for-green swap that is slightly beneficial or neutral to potential partners; Whale finishes with an attempted red-for-blue trade that benefits both possible recipients, producing multiple acceptances and a random partner selection (here realized as Dolphin). The game ends with holdings reflecting these incremental, self-interested exchanges.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players start with equal large endowments (10 of each chip). Each values green identically (0.50) but has different private values for red, blue, and purple. Proposers will typically request chips they privately value more and offer chips they value less; responders accept only when the offered-for-given exchange increases their own private-value holdings. Because proposals are open to both non-senders and the proposer cannot choose among multiple accepters, proposers face a trade-off between (a) crafting offers that are attractive to at least one partner (increasing execution probability) and (b) avoiding trades that transfer high-value chips to partners who would benefit more. Uncertainty stems from each player only knowing their own private valuations; they must anticipate which opponents will accept based on observable public history and common-range information. Given the player personas (cooperative, behaviorally stable, reflective), we forecast mostly small, mutually beneficial 1-for-1 exchanges that exploit clear value mismatches (e.g., a player who values purple highly will accept giving up red to receive purple only when that makes them better off), producing several straightforward accepted swaps across the three rounds.

rounds.round_explanation: Early in the game players are still symmetric in holdings but differ in private valuations. Proposers will choose small 1-for-1 swaps that exploit clear mismatches (e.g., a proposer with low purple value offering purple to obtain red from a player who values red less than purple). Responders accept when the net change in their private valuation is positive.

After a few successful swaps holdings shift but private values remain. Proposers continue to exploit clear asymmetric valuations: players with low purple value offer purple, those who value blue or red highly try to acquire them by offering green or purple. Multiple players can find some proposals attractive, creating occasional multi-acceptance outcomes resolved by random selection.

In the final round players push for remaining individually profitable trades. Proposers continue simple 1-for-1 offers that generate positive private-value gains for at least one partner. Where both non-senders stand to gain, multiple accepts occur and the final partner is determined randomly.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This 3-round, 3-player bargaining game pits private valuations against common starting endowments. Each player starts with 10 of each chip and can propose single-color-for-single-color trades; responders decide privately based only on their own chip values. Key incentives: proposers try to acquire chips they value more than what they give up; responders accept when the offered chips are worth at least as much to them as the chips they would surrender. Uncertainty comes from not knowing others' private values and from the random tie-break if multiple responders accept. Because green is common-valued (0.50) and other colors vary by player, many profitable proposals use green or low-valued personal chips as currency to attract at least one acceptor. Player personas bias choices: duck (cooperative, stable, numerate) tends to make straightforward value-improving offers and to propose clear, small exchanges; swan (reflective, tolerant of inequality) often accepts small positive gains and will make moderately attractive offers; whale (guarded, exploitation-cautious) demands clear proposer gains and prefers offers that produce a solid private benefit. Overall, we expect mostly small one-for-one exchanges that target the single responder whose private valuation yields a positive acceptance margin, with occasional offers that attract multiple acceptors (leading to random assignment).

rounds.round_explanation: Early play: players still have large symmetric holdings, so proposers try small 1-for-1 swaps that capitalize on differences in private values (e.g., duck values BLUE highly and PURPLE low). Proposers favor offers that will be attractive to at least one other player given their private valuations; responders accept when they gain positive value. There is also a risk of multiple acceptors when a common-value chip (GREEN) is involved.

Midgame: holdings have started to diverge. Duck continues to capitalize on his high BLUE valuation by buying BLUE with lower-valued chips; swan repeats a GREEN-for-BLUE move because it benefits him and is appealing to others; whale focuses on acquiring BLUE using PURPLE when that yields him clear gains. Many trades remain 1-for-1 and target the single responder for whom the exchange increases personal value.

Endgame: players with many of a high-valued color may still trade to exploit marginal gains. Duck uses RED to top up BLUE; swan continues swapping BLUE for GREEN when it benefits him and attracts others; whale keeps acquiring BLUE by offering PURPLE when a responder values the exchange positively. Toward the end, trades remain small and targeted so that at least one responder sees a direct personal gain.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, three-proposal-per-round bargaining game centers on heterogeneous private valuations for red, blue, and purple chips (green chips have a common value of $0.50). Each proposer can offer one color in exchange for another; non-senders accept only if the immediate exchange raises their own portfolio value given their private prices. Dynamics are driven by mutually beneficial bilateral gains: pig (highly values blue) will try to acquire blue, zebra (highly values red and purple) will trade toward red/purple, and swan (moderate red preference, lower purple) looks for straightforward positive-value swaps (often involving green as a neutral common-value asset). Uncertainty arises from not knowing others' private valuations (players only know their own), and from multi-accept cases where multiple partners may accept and one is randomly chosen. Expect quick, small mutually beneficial exchanges early (swapping colors that one player values more than another) and more declined offers when a proposer cannot create a deal that increases someone else’s payoff without harming them. Behavioral priors (pig: logical, moderately cooperative; zebra: cooperation-oriented but resource-protective; swan: empathic but variable) make players likely to propose gains for themselves that are also plausibly acceptable to others, with acceptance thresholds reflecting resource protection for zebra and variability for swan.

rounds.round_explanation: Round 0 begins from equal, public holdings (10 of each color per player). Early trades are likely to target clear mutual gains: pig wants blue (values it at $1.00) and can offer red (value $0.50 to pig); zebra and swan value blue less, so both could accept a favorable swap. Proposers will attempt simple 1-for-1 exchanges that create positive gains for both sides, but some offers (especially ones that give others net losses) will be declined.

Round 1 proceeds from updated public holdings after Round 0. Pig has accumulated extra blue and will continue to seek blue-for-cheap trades (selling low-valued purple). Zebra, with increased purple holdings, is willing to buy more purple by selling blue. Swan is selective and will decline exchanges that hurt her immediate value; she may occasionally propose trades converting low-value purple into green when it benefits both her and zebra.

Round 2 is the last opportunity to reshuffle holdings. Pig continues to exploit its comparative advantage in blue preference, proposing more blue-for-red swaps that create gains for both pig and at least one other player. Zebra keeps trading blue for purple when it yields a direct gain. Swan focuses on small, safe improvements (e.g., converting purple into green) that zebra may accept. Near-term immediate-value calculations dominate decisions, and several small mutually beneficial 1-for-1 trades are realized.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three risk- and value-driven players bargain over four chip types across three rounds with one proposal per player per round. Each proposer asks for one color and offers a different color; responders accept if the immediate exchange raises their own private chip-value sum. Key incentives: zebra and turtle strongly prize BLUE (1.00) and will try to acquire it when it is cheap to others; turtle also highly values RED (0.90), while swan values all non-BLUE chips roughly equally (≈0.50) and treats BLUE as the least valuable (0.40). Because green is a common-value chip (0.50) and purple has low private value for turtle (0.30), trades that move BLUE from swan to zebra/turtle, or move RED from zebra/swan to turtle, are mutually attractive in many pairings. Uncertainty arises from simultaneous, private accept/decline decisions and the random tie-break rule when multiple responders accept. Given the personas, zebra is calculated and stable, likely to propose profitable, low-friction one-for-one swaps; swan is structure-seeking and willing to trade away BLUE for other chips; turtle is cooperation-oriented but will accept exchanges that clearly raise her private value. Forecasted play therefore features repeated small one-for-one trades where proposers offer a chip they value less in exchange for chips they value more; acceptance decisions follow private-value comparisons and sometimes produce multiple acceptances resolved by chance.

rounds.round_explanation: Opening moves focus on inexpensive-to-others BLUE. Zebra, a calculated accumulator of value, will try to buy BLUE cheaply by offering GREEN (which zebra values at 0.50). Swan, who values BLUE only 0.40, is the natural seller. Responders accept if the swap improves their private payoff. Early trades are small (1-unit) and largely mutually beneficial, setting an expectation that swan is a willing BLUE seller.

Mid-game sees repetition of the profitable pattern: proposers exploit swan's relatively low value for BLUE and turtle's willingness to trade lower-value PURPLE or GREEN. Trades remain one-for-one; acceptance is driven by immediate private gains. Proposers also avoid over-offering (keep trades small), and responders often accept since the exchanges raise their own chip-value totals.

Endgame behavior is conservative but still value-seeking: players try one-last swaps to improve their private totals. Zebra continues to accumulate BLUE from swan. Swan repeatedly sells BLUE for reasonable tokens (RED or GREEN) because BLUE is her weakest chip. Turtle uses final turn to nudge holdings toward higher-value chips (obtaining BLUE by offering RED once marginally profitable). Because decisions are one-shot and responders do not coordinate, tie-break randomness can matter when multiple accept, but the forecasted trades here tend to produce single acceptances.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players repeatedly exchange single-color bundles to increase their own total chip value. Each player knows only their own per-chip values (kangaroo favors PURPLE strongly; frog favors BLUE strongly; unicorn favors RED and GREEN equally) and observes public holdings and past trades. Proposers will typically offer chips they value relatively less and request chips they value more; recipients accept only when the value they gain (value of offered chips) exceeds the value they give up (value of requested chips). Uncertainty arises because multiple non-senders can accept a proposal and the actual partner is then chosen at random, so proposers face partner-selection risk. Over three rounds (9 turns) we expect repeated, small-value-improving exchanges: frog and kangaroo trade around BLUE↔PURPLE swaps (frog values BLUE much more than PURPLE; kangaroo values PURPLE much more than BLUE), while unicorn opportunistically trades PURPLE or BLUE for RED (unicorn values RED highly relative to PURPLE). Because players act to maximize personal chip-value and have stable behavioral priors, trades tend to be conservative, small-quantity, and targeted to the counterparty who enjoys the offered color most, but occasional multi-accept events create random recipient outcomes.

rounds.round_explanation: Opening round: proposers look for easy, mutually beneficial exchanges that convert low-value holdings into higher-value chips. Kangaroo will try to buy PURPLE by offering BLUE (frog highly values BLUE); frog will try to accumulate BLUE by offering PURPLE; unicorn will attempt a small RED-for-PURPLE swap when it benefits a single partner. Because many players still hold many chips, small one- or two-chip proposals can be accepted by multiple players, creating random matching.

With updated holdings, proposers repeat the same comparative-advantage swaps. Kangaroo again prefers PURPLE and offers BLUE (both others may accept); frog continues to buy BLUE using PURPLE when kangaroo benefits; unicorn looks for single-partner RED acquisitions by offering BLUE when only one counterparty gains.

Final round: players consolidate gains with low-risk one-for-one swaps. Kangaroo trades remaining BLUE for GREEN with the player who values BLUE most (frog). Frog continues small BLUE-for-PURPLE trades with kangaroo when profitable; unicorn finishes by buying one more RED from the player who prefers BLUE (frog) by offering BLUE, yielding a straightforward acceptance.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players start with identical holdings (10 of each color) but privately value non-green chips differently; green chips have a common value (0.50). That creates clear trading motives: each proposer tends to offer chips they value relatively little and request chips they value relatively highly. Recipients decide solely on private gains (value received minus value given). Strategic features: proposers try to craft offers that look profitable to at least one other player given typical preference patterns (e.g., zebra strongly prefers BLUE, swan strongly prefers PURPLE, dolphin prefers RED/PURPLE), but they cannot pick among multiple acceptors (recipient chosen at random if more than one accept). Uncertainty arises from private valuations (proposers must predict others' acceptance using public ranges and prior tendencies) and from randomized partner selection when multiple accept. Player personas bias behavior: dolphin is generosity/cooperation-forward and may propose slightly generous, simple swaps; zebra is structure-seeking and will make clear value-improving offers; swan is reflective and selective but will accept straightforward profitable trades. Overall I forecast rapid, small-quantity swaps where low-value chips flow to players who value them more, with many mutually profitable 1-for-1 exchanges and occasional multi-unit offers that attract multiple acceptances.

rounds.round_explanation: Initial framing: everyone has symmetric holdings so proposers aim to convert their low-value chips into their high-value ones. Proposers will favor simple 1-for-1 (or small multi-unit) swaps that look plainly profitable to at least one other player given observed preference patterns. Early trades tend to be accepted by the player who gains most from the swap.

After the opening swaps, some players have slightly more of the chips they value. Proposers exploit remaining asymmetries by offering larger bundles when that makes acceptance very attractive to multiple recipients. Multiple acceptances can lead to randomized partner selection; proposers may deliberately offer multi-unit deals to increase the chance of any acceptance.

Endgame adjustments: with visible holdings altered by prior trades, proposers target remaining mismatches (e.g., pick off single high-value chips from holders who will accept small compensations). Trades tend toward 1-for-1 clean swaps; acceptance is driven by immediate private net gain since no future interaction is assumed.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, three-proposal-per-round bargaining game pits three players with heterogeneous private valuations over chip colors. Greens are common-value (0.50 to everyone) while red/blue/purple values differ by player: elephant favors blue (0.60) then red/green (0.50), hamster values green most (0.50) and treats red/blue equally (0.40), and turtle strongly prefers red (0.70). Each proposer will try to exchange chips they value relatively less for chips they value more; non-senders will accept only if the proposed exchange increases their own private total. Because players only know their own values, proposals that look attractive to a proposer may be rejected by some partners but accepted by others with different tastes (creating targeted offers). Random resolution (when multiple accept) and limited holdings generate uncertainty; players often craft modest, discrete exchanges that create a clear private surplus for one counterparty (e.g., offering low-value blue in return for high-value red to a red-lover). The player personas bias behavior: elephant (cooperative, logical) will propose trades that modestly increase his value and try to signal acceptability; hamster (short-horizon, exploitation-cautious) will favor sure, quick gains and may propose safer small arbitrage; turtle (red-seeking, reflective) will aggressively convert low-value chips (blue/purple) into red when partners are likely to accept. Across the three rounds, expect a mix of accepted targeted trades (where the recipient strictly benefits) and some declined proposals when no counterparty strictly benefits, with holdings updating immediately and shaping later offers.

rounds.round_explanation: Early trades focus on converting low-value purple/blue into each player's high-preference chips. Proposers try small, profitable swaps that create clear gains for one other player (targeting turtle's red preference or turtle's willingness to trade away blue). Expect some mutually beneficial asymmetric trades to execute quickly; other offers that would hurt all recipients will be declined.

With updated holdings, proposers look for marginal improvements. Elephant continues converting lower-value chips into more-preferred colors (targeting the player who benefits most). Hamster attempts a modest arbitrage but may fail if no counterparty strictly benefits; turtle continues to exploit situations where others value the offered chip more than the requested chip.

Final-round proposals aim for small sure gains given updated positions. Elephant makes a compact swap that benefits a partner (trading a green for a blue); hamster and turtle seek one-step improvements consistent with their private values and earlier behavior. Expect targeted accepts where a single recipient clearly benefits; otherwise proposals are declined.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players repeatedly propose bilateral chip exchanges where a proposer offers some chips and requests other chips. Each player knows only their own private valuations for red/blue/purple (green is common-value), so proposals are aimed at trades that increase the proposer's value while offering enough compensation that at least one other player gains from the swap. Key incentives: players trade away chips they value relatively less in exchange for chips they value relatively more; because acceptance is required from non-senders, a proposer must target partners who incur a (weakly) positive net gain from the swap. Uncertainty arises from (1) private valuations of red/blue/purple (others' preferences are unknown to the proposer), (2) simultaneous private accept/decline responses which can lead to multiple acceptances and a random recipient, and (3) sequencing effects because holdings change after each accepted trade. Personae steer behavior: dog-green-9823 aggressively pursues high-value blue/red but is willing to pay green or small comps; badger-pink-6314 is relatively prosocial/tolerant and may accept marginally fair deals; cat-blue-2956 is cautious about exploitation but will sell low-value chips (red/blue) to obtain greener chips. The result is a mix of declined exploratory offers and several transactions where low-value chips move to players who value them more and green (common-value) moves to players who prize it or who are paid to give it up.

rounds.round_explanation: Early-game signaling and information gathering. Proposers test which partners will trade by offering modest swaps that convert low-value chips into higher-value ones for themselves. Responders accept when the offered compensation exceeds their private cost. Expect some declined exploratory offers followed by at least one clear mutually beneficial exchange.

With more public history and updated holdings, proposers craft trades that are more likely to be accepted. Badger leverages low-valued purple to acquire green, dog targets blue with a straightforward green-for-blue swap, and cat continues to trade low-value red for green when the price is acceptable to at least one partner.

Endgame trades focus on consolidating high-valued chips. Badger purchases red using blue, dog opportunistically converts a low-value purple into an extra blue, and cat continues trading away red for green. Because holdings and known responses are now visible, proposers make offers that target likely acceptors.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players start with equal chip counts but private, asymmetric values for red/blue/purple (greens are common-value). Each proposer seeks trades that increase their own total value; because players only know their own valuations, proposers generally offer chips they value relatively less and request chips they value more.
